1.html:
Two people look at the same stock on the same morning. One has read the company's annual report cover to cover — the profit margins, the debt, the management's track record — and is convinced the business is worth far more than today's price. The other knows almost none of that, and may not even be sure what the company sells. This second person is watching exactly two things: the line of prices the stock has printed over the past year, and how many shares changed hands at each step. Both can make money. Both can lose. This course is about the craft of the second person.

Technical analysis has a reputation problem. To its fans it is a secret language that predicts the future; to its critics it is astrology with a trading terminal. Both are wrong, and the truth is far more useful than either. Technical analysis is the disciplined study of price and volume to tilt the odds — never the certainty — slightly in your favour, and then to manage the risk when you turn out to be wrong. That last clause is where the real money lives, and most of this course is secretly about it.

Price and volume: the only two inputs

Strip technical analysis down to the metal and you find just two raw materials. Price — what the stock last traded at — and volume — how many shares traded to get there. Every tool you will meet in this course, from a simple trendline to a Bollinger Band, is nothing more than arithmetic performed on those two streams. Trends, support levels, candlesticks, moving averages, RSI, MACD: all of them are derived numbers. Price and volume are the source.

Why lean on so little? Because price is the one number on which every participant on earth has already voted, with real money on the line. It already contains the optimist who read the annual report and bought, the fund manager rebalancing, the trader cutting a loss, and the person who just got laid off and needs the cash today. You will never know each of their reasons. You do not need to. The chart is the running tally of that vote.

Key idea

A price is not an opinion about a stock — it is the settled consensus of everyone who has bought or sold it, weighted by how much they traded. Technical analysis is the study of how that consensus forms, holds, and breaks. You are reading the crowd's behaviour, not predicting the company's future.

Technical analysis versus fundamental analysis

The cleanest way to understand technical analysis is to set it beside its older sibling. A fundamental analyst asks one question: what is this business actually worth? They dig into earnings, cash flow, debt, competitive moats and the quality of management, estimate an intrinsic value, and try to buy when the price sits below it. Their unit of analysis is the company.

A technical analyst asks a different question: what is the price doing, and what are the odds it keeps doing it? They read the footprints that supply and demand leave on the chart — where buyers showed up, where sellers overwhelmed them, whether the trend is gaining or losing steam. Their unit of analysis is the price action itself.

These are different questions on different clocks, not enemies. Fundamentals tend to answer what to own and roughly why, over months and years. Technicals tend to help with when to act and how to manage the position, over days and weeks. Plenty of serious desks use both: fundamentals to choose the shortlist of stocks worth owning, and technicals to time the entry and define the exit. You do not have to pick a religion. You have to know which question each tool is built to answer.

The three assumptions it rests on

Technical analysis stands on three load-bearing assumptions. Knowing them — and their limits — is what separates a thoughtful chartist from someone drawing hopeful lines.

  1. Price discounts everything. All knowable information — earnings, news, rumour, fear, even the trade the insider placed before the announcement — is already reflected in the price. The chartist's claim is not that fundamentals do not matter, but that they have already been voted into the tape. You read the result, not the cause.
  2. Prices move in trends. Once a direction is established, it is more likely to persist than to reverse at random — until something genuinely changes. This single assumption is why trend-following exists, and why "the trend is your friend" survived a century of mockery.
  3. History rhymes. Markets are made of people, and human behaviour — the rush to grab a quick profit, the refusal to accept a loss, the herd piling in late — repeats. So the patterns those behaviours stamp onto a chart tend to recur. Not identically. They rhyme.
Heads up

These are assumptions, not laws of physics. Markets violate all three regularly — trends end, prices ignore obvious news, and the pattern that "always" worked stops working the month you start trading it. The skill is to treat each assumption as a tendency that pays over many trades, never a guarantee on the next one.

What a chart can — and cannot — predict

Be honest about the ceiling. A chart cannot tell you tomorrow's price, cannot guarantee that a textbook pattern will play out, and cannot rescue a position with no risk plan. Anyone selling you those things is selling.

What a chart genuinely can do is narrower but real: it shows you the direction and health of the current trend, marks the levels where the crowd has reacted strongly before, flags the moments when the odds of a move shift, and — most valuable of all — tells you the exact price at which your idea was wrong, so you can leave. The honest frame for the entire field is probability, not prophecy. A setup that works six times in ten is excellent. It still fails four times in ten, and you cannot know in advance which trade you are in.

Did you know

We did not take the textbook's word for any of this. Every quantitative claim in this course was measured on 16 years of real NSE daily data — the Nifty 50, 49 stocks, 2010 to 2026, 192,786 daily bars. The results are often uncomfortable:

  • The famous golden cross buy signal was positive 60 days later only 59% of the time — barely better than the market's everyday drift.
  • The dreaded death cross sell signal was actually higher 60 days later 66% of the time — more often than the golden cross.
  • The most "overbought" stocks (RSI above 80), which textbooks scream at you to sell, posted the best forward returns of any group we tested.

We will show the workings chapter by chapter. For now, absorb the method: measure, don't believe.

Where technical analysis actually adds an edge

If the popular signals barely beat doing nothing, why bother? Because the edge was never really in the signal. Start with a number that reframes everything: across our data, the average Nifty 50 stock drifted up about +1.48% over any 20 trading days, simply because the market floats higher over time. That baseline is the silent benchmark. Almost any "signal" looks like it "works" — until you compare it to just being invested, at which point most of the magic evaporates.

The real edge lives in three unglamorous places. First, risk control: a chart gives you objective, pre-decided places to exit when you are wrong, which keeps losses small enough to survive. Second, consistency: rules you can repeat beat moods you cannot. Third, framing and timing: when the fundamentals say buy, the chart can stop you from buying into a falling knife. Notice that none of these is prediction.

Tip

Train one reflex above all others as you read this course: ask "compared to what?" A signal that is right 60% of the time is only worth using if simply holding the stock was not right 58% of the time anyway. Base rates first, signals second. This one habit will protect you from most of the nonsense in technical analysis.

The thread that runs through all 25 chapters is this: the indicator is rarely the edge. The discipline around it is. We will give you the tools honestly — what each one measures, where it helps, and the exact data on where it fails — and then spend the back half of the course on the risk management and psychology that actually keep traders solvent.

Quick recap

  • Technical analysis is the disciplined study of price and volume to shift the odds in your favour and control risk — not to predict the future.
  • A fundamental analyst asks what a business is worth; a technical analyst asks what the price is doing and whether it is likely to continue. They answer different questions and pair well.
  • TA rests on three assumptions — price discounts everything, prices move in trends, history rhymes — which are tendencies, not laws.
  • Charts deal in probability, not prophecy: they show trend, levels and where you are wrong, but cannot promise an outcome.
  • The market's baseline drift (about +1.48% per 20 days) is the benchmark every signal must beat — and many barely do.
  • The durable edge is in risk control, consistency and discipline, not in any single indicator. We tested every claim here on 16 years of real Nifty 50 data.

Next, we open the thing all of this is built on — the price chart itself — and learn to read it without getting lost.

2.html:
Open any trading platform for the first time and the chart can feel like a wall of noise — a jagged red-and-green forest with numbers crawling up the side and dates along the bottom. Experienced traders look at the very same screen and read it like a sentence. The difference is not talent or a faster eye. It is knowing what each mark on the chart is, and learning to ignore the ninety percent that does not matter today.

A price chart only ever shows two things: price, on the vertical axis, and time, on the horizontal one. Everything else is a choice about how to draw the price — as a line, as a bar, or as a candlestick. Those three styles plot identical data and yet tell the story with very different richness. Get them straight and the wall of noise turns into information.

Three ways to draw the same price

Imagine a single trading day for a stock. It opened at one price, traded as high as another, dipped as low as a third, and finally closed at a fourth. That is four numbers for one day. The three chart styles differ entirely in how many of those four numbers they bother to show you.

A line chart is the simplest: it plots only the closing price of each period and connects the dots. It throws away three of the four numbers. That sounds wasteful, but the close is the most important price of the day — it is where the market settled after everyone had their say — so a line chart is wonderful for seeing the big-picture direction without distraction. It is the wrong tool the moment you need to know how the price got there.

A bar chart (often called an OHLC bar) keeps all four numbers. Each period is a vertical stick spanning the low to the high, with a small tick on the left for the open and a tick on the right for the close. It is complete and compact, and it was the professional's chart for decades.

A candlestick chart shows the exact same four numbers as a bar, but draws the distance between the open and the close as a filled or hollow rectangle — the body — with thin lines above and below for the extremes. That single design choice, which we will dissect next chapter, makes the balance of buyers and sellers jump off the screen. It is why almost every trader today reads candles.

OHLC: the four numbers in every bar

Whatever style you choose, the underlying data for each period is the same four prices, always in the same order:

Field Meaning
Open The first traded price of the period
High The highest price reached during the period
Low The lowest price reached during the period
Close The last traded price of the period

This is the famous OHLC. On a daily chart each bar is one trading day; on a weekly chart, one week. The four numbers are not decoration — they are the entire factual record of what happened. When you pull data with a tool like OpenAlgo, these are exactly the columns you get back, one row per period:

from openalgo import api

client = api(api_key="your_key")
df = client.history(symbol="RELIANCE", exchange="NSE",
                    interval="D", start_date="2025-12-01", end_date="2026-06-25")
print(df[["open", "high", "low", "close", "volume"]].tail())

Notice the fifth column, volume — the number of shares traded in the period. Price tells you what happened; volume tells you how convincingly. We will give volume a chapter of its own, but keep it in the corner of your eye from the start.

Reading the axes

Two axes, two jobs. The vertical (price) axis on the right or left tells you the rupee level of every point on the chart. The horizontal (time) axis along the bottom tells you when each bar occurred. They sound trivial, and that is exactly why they trip people up — the axes are where a chart quietly misleads you, a problem we devote a whole chapter to later. For now, two habits: always check what period one bar represents (a daily bar and a 5-minute bar look identical at a glance), and always read the price scale before reacting to a "huge" move, because a chart squeezed into a narrow range can make a one-percent wiggle look like a crash.

Tip

Before you form any opinion about a chart, answer three questions out loud: What stock is this? What is one bar — a day, a week, a minute? What is the price range top to bottom? Skipping this is the single most common way beginners misread a perfectly normal chart as dramatic.

Why candlesticks win

If a bar and a candle carry identical numbers, why has the candle taken over? Because of how fast the human eye reads it. A candlestick uses colour and a solid body to encode the one thing traders care about most: did buyers or sellers win this period, and by how much?

When the close is above the open, the body is drawn hollow or green — buyers were in control. When the close is below the open, the body is filled or red — sellers won. The length of the body shows the margin of victory at a glance, and the thin wicks above and below show how far price travelled before being pushed back. You can scan fifty candles in a second and feel the rhythm of a battle. The same fifty OHLC bars require you to compare left-tick and right-tick positions one by one. Same data, far lower effort — and in a fast market, effort is everything.

Key idea

A line chart shows you where price went. A candlestick shows you how hard it was fought over to get there. That extra layer — open versus close, and the rejection stored in the wicks — is why candlesticks dominate modern charting and why the next chapter is devoted entirely to reading a single candle.

Reading a real daily chart

Theory is cheap. Here is an actual daily candlestick chart of RELIANCE, 140 trading days from December 2025 into June 2026.

Read it the way the checklist taught you. The stock is RELIANCE; each bar is one trading day; the price scale runs from roughly 1,253 at the bottom to 1,612 at the top — a span of under 30%, so this is a normal, liquid large-cap doing normal things, not a crisis. Now the story: price pushed up to a high near 1,612, then spent the back half of the window working lower, ending around 1,318 — meaningfully below where it peaked. No prediction required to see it; the chart simply records that buyers ran out of room near the top and sellers had the upper hand into the close of the period. A line chart of the same data would show you that drift down. The candles add the texture — which days were fought hard, which gave up easily — that we will learn to read next.

Common chart-reading mistakes

A few errors trip up almost everyone at the start. Name them now and you will catch yourself doing them.

  • Reacting before reading the scale. A move that looks violent is often one percent on a compressed axis. Read the price range first.
  • Confusing the timeframe. A daily and an intraday chart look the same; the conclusions are not. Always know what one bar is.
  • Treating the latest bar as the whole story. A single red candle is one day in a year. Zoom out before you decide anything.
  • Seeing patterns in pure noise. The eye is a pattern-making machine and will find a "head and shoulders" in random wiggle. Demand context — a later chapter is entirely about this trap.
  • Forgetting volume exists. Price without volume is half the picture. Glance at the bars underneath.
Heads up

The chart is a record, not a forecast. Everything to the left of the latest bar already happened; everything to the right is unknown. Reading a chart well means describing what has occurred accurately and honestly — not narrating a confident story about what comes next.

Quick recap

  • A chart plots price (vertical) against time (horizontal); everything else is a choice of how to draw the price.
  • Line charts show only the close (great for direction), bar charts show all four OHLC prices, and candlesticks show the same four but make the buyer-versus-seller balance visible at a glance.
  • Every bar carries Open, High, Low, Close — plus Volume — and on a daily chart one bar is one trading day.
  • Candlesticks dominate because colour and body length let the eye read the outcome of each period instantly.
  • On the real RELIANCE chart, price ran to about 1,612 then faded to close near 1,318 — strong stocks give gains back too.
  • The classic mistakes are reacting before reading the scale, confusing the timeframe, and over-weighting the latest bar — fix these first.

Next, we zoom all the way in to a single candlestick and learn how five numbers and one shape tell a complete story of conviction, rejection and doubt.

3.html:
Zoom all the way in on any chart, past the trends and the levels, and you reach the atom of technical analysis: a single candlestick. It looks like a tiny box with threads poking out of each end. It is, in fact, a compressed story — a whole trading day's worth of human hope, fear and second-guessing, packed into one shape you can read in under a second. Learn to read that one shape and every chart in this course becomes a sentence built from a vocabulary you already know.

The candlestick is a Japanese invention, centuries older than the computer, and it survived because it does one thing brilliantly: it shows you not just where price ended up, but who was in control on the way there. A line on a chart says "the price was here." A candle says "buyers tried to push it up, sellers slammed it back from this level, and by the bell the buyers had won — narrowly." That is a different, richer kind of information, and it is free in every bar.

Five numbers, one shape

Each bar of data carries five numbers: the open, high, low and close (the OHLC you met last chapter), plus the volume traded. The candlestick draws four of them as a picture and leaves volume to a separate row beneath. Those four prices define the entire shape:

  • The open and close mark the two ends of the thick rectangle in the middle.
  • The high and low mark the tips of the thin threads above and below.

Two numbers for the box, two for the threads. That is the whole construction. From four prices, a shape; from the shape, a story.

# Every candle's geometry comes straight from the OHLC columns
df["body"]       = (df["close"] - df["open"]).abs()   # size of the rectangle
df["upper_wick"] = df["high"] - df[["open", "close"]].max(axis=1)
df["lower_wick"] = df[["open", "close"]].min(axis=1) - df["low"]

You will rarely compute these by hand — your charting tool draws them for you — but seeing the arithmetic makes it concrete: the candle is not a symbol someone decided meant "buy." It is a faithful, mechanical drawing of four real prices.

Anatomy: body, wick and shadow

Give the parts their names, because the rest of the course assumes them.

The body is the thick rectangle between the open and the close. Its length is the single most important feature of a candle: it measures how far price travelled, net, from start to finish — the margin of victory for whichever side won.

The wicks — also called shadows — are the thin lines extending above and below the body. The upper wick reaches up to the high; the lower wick reaches down to the low. They record the ground that price covered intraday but could not hold. A long wick is a story of a push that got rejected.

That is the entire vocabulary: body, upper wick, lower wick. Everything else — every named pattern in Module C — is just combinations of these three parts across one, two or three candles.

Colour: bullish versus bearish

Colour answers one question instantly: did the period close higher or lower than it opened?

A bullish candle closes above its open — buyers ended in control. It is conventionally drawn green or hollow. A bearish candle closes below its open — sellers ended in control — and is drawn red or filled. The colour is not about whether the stock rose versus yesterday; it is strictly open-versus-close within this one period. A stock can gap down hard at the open and still print a green candle if buyers clawed it back by the close. That nuance — green does not always mean "up day" — is exactly the kind of thing the colour reveals and a line chart hides.

Key idea

The body answers two questions at once: its colour says who won (close above or below open), and its length says by how much. A long green body is decisive buying; a long red body is decisive selling; a tiny body of either colour is a near-draw. Before you name any pattern, just read the body — it is most of the message.

Long wicks: the language of rejection

If the body is conviction, the wicks are rejection — and they are where candlesticks earn their reputation. A long wick means price travelled a long way in one direction during the period and was then forcefully pushed back before the close.

A long lower wick says: sellers drove the price down, buyers came in underneath, absorbed the selling, and lifted it back up by the bell. Buyers defended that lower zone. A long upper wick says the opposite: buyers pushed the price up, ran into a wall of selling, and were shoved back down. Sellers defended that upper zone. The longer the wick relative to the body, the more emphatic the rejection. A candle with a small body and a long wick is one of the most informative single bars you can see, because it shows a clear shift of control within the period.

Tip

When a long wick appears, ask where it appeared, not just that it appeared. A long lower wick stabbing into a level where the stock has bounced before is meaningful. The identical candle in the middle of empty space, with no level nearby, is mostly noise. The shape is the same; the context is everything — a point we build an entire chapter around later.

Marubozu and doji: the two extremes

Two named single candles sit at opposite ends of the conviction scale, and they are worth knowing now.

A marubozu is a candle that is almost all body — little or no wick at either end. It opened at one extreme and closed at the other, marching in one direction the entire period with no meaningful pushback. A green marubozu is pure, one-sided buying; a red one is pure, one-sided selling. It is the chart's clearest expression of conviction: one side was in control from the first tick to the last.

A doji is the mirror image: a candle with almost no body at all, because the open and the close finished at nearly the same price. Buyers and sellers fought all period and ended in a draw. A doji is the chart's clearest expression of indecision — a balance of power, a pause, a question mark. After a long run in one direction, that sudden balance can hint the trend is tiring. Hint is the operative word.

Heads up

Resist the urge to turn a single candle into a trade. A doji does not "mean reversal" and a hammer does not "mean buy" — not on its own. One candle is a single data point in an ongoing fight, and the data shows clearly that isolated patterns barely beat doing nothing. A single candle can raise a question; only the surrounding trend, level and volume can begin to answer it. We will measure exactly how weak lone patterns are when we reach the candlestick-pattern chapters.

Size and context: a candle whispers, it does not shout

Two final habits turn candle-reading from a parlour trick into a skill.

First, read size relative to its neighbours, not in the abstract. A "big" candle only matters if it is big compared with the recent bars. A long green body that dwarfs the last ten candles signals a real surge of demand; the same body in a stock that swings that much every day is unremarkable. Always judge a candle against its own recent range.

Second, remember that a single candle whispers. It tells you who won one round and how decisively — genuinely useful information — but it cannot tell you the fight's outcome. The story of a chart is told by candles in sequence: a long green body is more meaningful after a string of weak red ones; a doji counts for more at the top of a long climb than in the middle of a quiet drift. You are learning the alphabet here. The words and sentences — two- and three-candle patterns, and patterns read in full context — come in Module C, and they only make sense once a lone candle is second nature.

Quick recap

  • A candlestick draws four of a bar's five numbers — open, high, low, close — as a shape; volume sits in a separate row.
  • The body (open-to-close) shows conviction by its length and direction by its colour; the wicks (to the high and low) show how far price was pushed and rejected.
  • Green/hollow closes above the open (buyers won); red/filled closes below (sellers won) — strictly within that one period, not versus yesterday.
  • A long wick is rejection — buyers or sellers defending a zone; a marubozu is one-sided conviction; a doji is indecision.
  • Judge candle size against recent bars, and treat any single candle as a whisper — context (trend, level, volume) decides whether it matters.
  • Isolated candle patterns barely beat doing nothing; their power, where it exists, comes only from where they appear.

Next, we pull back to ask a question that quietly decides everything you have just learned to read: which timeframe and scale are you looking at — and how the same chart can tell opposite stories depending on the answer.

4.html:
Here is an uncomfortable trick you can play on yourself. Pull up a stock on a 15-minute chart and it looks like it is collapsing — every recent bar red, the line falling off a cliff. Switch the very same stock to a weekly chart and it looks magnificent — a clean staircase of higher highs marching up the page. Nothing about the company changed in the half-second it took to click. Only the timeframe changed. Both pictures are true. They are simply answering different questions.

This is the chapter where charts stop being innocent. The candle you learned to read does not have a fixed meaning — its meaning depends entirely on the timeframe it is drawn on and the scale it is drawn against. A trader who does not control those two settings is not reading the market; the chart's defaults are reading it for them. So before any indicator or pattern, you have to master the frame around the picture.

One stock, many timeframes

A timeframe is simply how much trading each bar represents. On a daily chart, one candle is one trading day. On a weekly chart, one candle bundles a whole week into a single open, high, low and close. On a monthly chart, one candle is an entire month. The price data underneath is identical — you are just choosing how coarsely to slice it.

Think of it like a map. The monthly chart is the country-level view — it shows the long, slow tide of where a stock has been over years. The weekly chart is the state-level view — the multi-week swings that make up that tide. The daily chart is the city-level view — close enough to see individual days fight it out. None of these is "the real" chart. They are zoom levels, and a complete picture needs more than one, which is exactly why a later chapter is devoted to reading several at once.

How the timeframe flips the signal

The reason this matters so much is that a signal on one timeframe can be the opposite of the signal on another — and both can be correct. A stock in a powerful multi-month uptrend on the weekly chart will, within that climb, have plenty of ugly red days on the daily chart. Those red days are not the trend reversing; they are the small, normal pullbacks that every uptrend is made of. A trader staring only at the daily chart sees danger. A trader who checked the weekly first sees a dip inside strength.

Heads up

A bearish-looking lower timeframe inside a bullish higher timeframe is the single most common reason new traders get shaken out of good positions — and talked into bad ones. The fix is not a better indicator. It is the discipline to always know which timeframe you are reacting to, and to check the bigger one before you trust the smaller one.

This cuts both ways. Day traders deliberately live on intraday charts because they want to catch moves too small to register on a daily bar. Long-term investors deliberately ignore the daily noise and act on weekly or monthly signals. Neither is wrong. What is wrong is mixing them by accident — taking a long-term decision based on a five-minute panic, or a quick trade based on a multi-year trend. The timeframe must match your intent.

Key idea

Higher timeframes set the direction; lower timeframes set the timing. The weekly tells you which way the tide is running; the daily tells you when to step in. When they disagree, the higher timeframe usually wins the argument — and trading against it is swimming against the tide.

Why this course uses daily data

With so many timeframes available, this course deliberately teaches on daily charts, and the choice is not arbitrary.

Daily bars sit at the sweet spot for learning. They are slow enough to be calm — one decision point per day, not hundreds — so you can actually think, and so a pattern has room to mean something. Yet they are fast enough that a meaningful sample of signals accumulates over a few years, which is what let us measure every claim in this course. Intraday data, by contrast, is dominated by noise and microstructure quirks that drown out the simple price behaviour a beginner needs to see. And every quantitative finding we cite — the golden-cross base rates, the gap-fill rates, the RSI buckets — was computed on 16 years of real daily Nifty 50 bars, because daily data over that long a window gives honest, repeatable statistics rather than a handful of lucky anecdotes.

Master the daily chart and the skills transfer cleanly. A candle is a candle, a trend is a trend, support is support — whether the bar is a day, a week or a minute. Learn the grammar on the daily timeframe, where it is clearest, and you can apply it anywhere later.

Linear versus logarithmic scale

The second hidden setting is the price axis itself, and it fools people for years without their noticing.

A linear (arithmetic) scale spaces prices by equal rupee amounts: the gap from 100 to 200 looks exactly the same as the gap from 1,000 to 1,100. Both are 100 rupees. A logarithmic (log) scale spaces prices by equal percentage amounts: the gap from 100 to 200 (a 100% gain) looks the same as the gap from 1,000 to 2,000 (also 100%). For a single day's chart the two are indistinguishable. Over years of compounding, they tell wildly different stories.

Consider a real example: BEL ran from about 175 to 468 over roughly two years — a gain of around 168%. On a linear chart, the early move from 175 to 250 looks like a flat, boring crawl near the bottom, while the later move from 400 to 468 looks like a dramatic surge, even though in percentage terms the early move was far bigger and far more important to anyone who owned it from the start. The linear axis literally hides the strongest part of the trend and exaggerates the weakest. A log chart fixes this: equal percentage moves get equal vertical space, so a steady trend looks like a steady straight line and you can compare the slope of the early gains with the late ones fairly.

Tip

Use a logarithmic scale whenever a chart spans a large percentage range — a multi-year stock chart, or any asset that has multiplied several times over. Use a linear scale for short windows or tight ranges where percentage and rupee moves are nearly the same. Getting this one setting right will change how a long-term trend looks, and stop you from mistaking ordinary early gains for a flat base.

How settings quietly distort a chart

Beyond timeframe and scale, a handful of innocent-looking display settings can quietly change the story a chart tells. Knowing them keeps you honest.

  • A truncated price axis. If the vertical axis does not start near a sensible reference and instead zooms into a narrow band, a one-percent wobble can be stretched to look like a crash. Always read the actual numbers on the axis before reacting to the shape.
  • Auto-scaling. Most platforms re-fit the axis to whatever is on screen. Scroll or change the date range and the same candles suddenly look bigger or smaller. The price did not move; the frame did.
  • The chosen date window. Start a chart at a crash low and everything looks like a heroic recovery. Start it at a bubble peak and the same stock looks broken. The window you pick is an editorial choice — pick it honestly.
  • Aggregation boundaries. A weekly candle depends on where the week is cut. The same days grouped differently can change which weekly candles look bullish or bearish. It is a reminder that even the bars are a construction, not a fact of nature.
Heads up

None of these settings is dishonest by design — but together they mean two traders can look at the "same" stock and see opposite things purely because of timeframe, scale and window. Whenever a chart provokes a strong reaction in you, check the frame first. Half the time the drama is in the settings, not the stock.

Choosing the right timeframe

So which timeframe should you use? The honest answer is the one that matches how long you intend to hold and how often you intend to act. A long-term investor checking in weekly has no business reacting to a 15-minute chart. A short-term swing trader holding for days anchors on the daily chart and perhaps glances at the weekly for direction. An intraday trader works on minutes but should still know where the daily trend points. The principle is constant: decide your holding period first, then pick the timeframe that fits it — never the other way around. Letting an exciting intraday chart talk you into a long-term decision (or vice versa) is how timeframes mislead.

Quick recap

  • A timeframe is how much trading each bar holds — daily, weekly, monthly are zoom levels on the same price history, like country, state and city maps.
  • The same stock can look bullish on one timeframe and bearish on another, and both can be true; higher timeframes set direction, lower ones set timing.
  • This course teaches on daily charts because they are calm enough to think on, rich enough to measure, and where every statistic here was computed (16 years of Nifty 50 data).
  • A linear axis spaces equal rupees; a logarithmic axis spaces equal percentages — use log for long, large-percentage moves (a stock running ~175 to 468 looks honest only on a log scale).
  • Truncated axes, auto-scaling, the date window and aggregation can all quietly change the story — check the frame before reacting.
  • Pick your timeframe from your holding period, not from whichever chart looks most exciting.

With the chart, the candle, the timeframe and the scale all under control, you are ready for structure. Next, we meet the 120-year-old framework — Dow Theory — that quietly underpins everything chartists still do today.

toc.text: How the timeframe flips the signal
Why this course uses daily data
How settings quietly distort a chart

5.html:
Before there were screens, scanners or a single line of Python, there was a newspaper editor in New York quietly writing down what the market was doing. Charles Dow never published a "method" — he died in 1902 with his ideas scattered across editorials in the paper he co-founded. His followers stitched those editorials into what we now call Dow Theory, and more than a century later almost everything you will learn in this course is a footnote to it. Trends, phases, confirmation, the idea that price already knows more than you do — Dow said it first.

That is why we start here. Not out of respect for a dead man, but because Dow Theory is the grammar of charts. Once you can see a market in Dow's terms — a primary direction, a counter-move against it, volume agreeing or disagreeing — every indicator later in the course becomes a way of measuring something you can already see with your eyes. Learn the grammar first, and the vocabulary takes care of itself.

The six tenets

Dow Theory is usually distilled into six principles. None of them is a buy signal. Together they are a way of describing a market honestly.

  1. The averages discount everything. Every known hope, fear, earnings forecast and rumour is already in the price. You are not smarter than the collective bid and offer; you are trying to read it.
  2. The market has three trends. A primary trend lasting months to years, a secondary reaction lasting weeks, and minor noise lasting days. They are nested inside each other like waves, ripples and chop on the sea.
  3. Primary trends have three phases — accumulation, public participation, and distribution. We will walk through these below.
  4. The averages must confirm each other. Dow watched the Industrials and the Railroads. If goods were being made (Industrials rising) they had to be shipped (Railroads rising). When one average made a new high and the other refused, he distrusted the move. The modern echo is checking whether a broad index and its sector or breadth agree.
  5. Volume must confirm the trend. Price is the what; volume is the how convincingly. A rally on shrinking volume is suspect.
  6. A trend is assumed in force until a clear reversal. You do not anticipate the turn — you wait for the structure itself to break.
Key idea

The whole of Dow Theory reduces to one stubborn idea: assume the existing trend continues until the chart proves otherwise. Everything else — phases, confirmation, volume — is just how you decide the chart has proven otherwise.

Three trends, nested inside each other

The single most useful thing Dow gave us is the idea that several trends exist at the same time, and confusion comes from mixing them up.

  • The primary trend is the tide. It runs for months or years and is the one that actually builds or destroys wealth. A position trader cares mostly about this.
  • The secondary trend is the wave moving against the tide — a pullback in a bull market, a relief bounce in a bear market. These last weeks and routinely retrace a third to two-thirds of the previous primary move. They terrify people into thinking the tide has turned. Usually it hasn't.
  • The minor trend is the daily chop — ripples on the wave. Dow considered it largely noise and easily manipulated. A swing trader uses it for timing; a long-term investor ignores it.

The practical lesson is to always know which trend you are reacting to. A 6% drop is a catastrophe if it is the start of a new primary downtrend and a gift if it is an ordinary secondary pullback inside a healthy uptrend. The chart, not your stomach, tells you which.

Higher highs and higher lows

Dow defined a trend not by a feeling but by structure — the sequence of peaks and troughs.

  • An uptrend is a staircase of higher highs and higher lows (HH/HL). Each rally exceeds the last peak; each dip stops above the last trough.
  • A downtrend is lower highs and lower lows (LH/LL). Each bounce fails below the prior peak; each decline undercuts the prior low.
  • A sideways market makes roughly equal highs and lows — no staircase, just a corridor.

The uptrend is intact as long as that pattern holds. The first crack is not a single red candle — it is the day a pullback breaks below the previous higher low. That is the structural event that says "the staircase may be turning," and it matters far more than any indicator flashing a colour.

from openalgo import ta

# A 50-day moving average is a crude, objective read on primary direction:
# rising line and price above it = the staircase is still climbing.
df["sma50"] = ta.sma(df["close"], 50)
Tip

You can mark structure by hand on any chart: tag each swing high and swing low, then just read the sequence out loud — "higher high, higher low, higher high..." The moment that sentence breaks, your trend assumption is on notice. No indicator required.

The four phases of a primary trend

Dow described a bull market in three phases; pairing it with the bear market that follows gives the full cycle traders still draw today: accumulation → markup → distribution → markdown.

  • Accumulation. The smart, patient money buys while the news is still grim and everyone else is exhausted. Price goes sideways in a base; volume is dull. Nobody is excited — which is precisely the point.
  • Markup. The trend becomes obvious. Higher highs and higher lows stack up, volume expands on the up-days, and trend-followers and the financial press climb aboard. This is the longest, most profitable phase and the easiest to actually trade.
  • Distribution. The same informed money now sells into the euphoria. Price stops making clean new highs, churns sideways on heavy volume, and the headlines are at their most bullish. It mirrors accumulation, just with the crowd on the wrong side.
  • Markdown. Supply wins. Lower highs and lower lows take over, and the people who bought the top hold on hoping for a return to even — supplying the selling pressure that drives the next leg down.

You will not label these phases live with any certainty; they are clearest in hindsight. But the framework keeps you honest about where in the cycle a market probably is, which is more than most people who chase a chart ever ask.

Why volume must confirm

Volume is the lie-detector of Dow Theory. In a healthy uptrend, volume should expand when price moves with the primary trend and shrink on the counter-trend pullbacks. When that relationship inverts — rallies on thinning volume, declines on swelling volume — the trend is running on fumes even if price is still ticking up.

Heads up

Volume confirmation is a warning system, not a trigger. Plenty of trends climb a wall of mediocre volume and keep going; plenty of volume spikes lead nowhere. Treat a volume divergence as a reason to tighten risk and pay attention — never as a guaranteed reversal.

How Dow Theory shows up today

Strip away the railroad averages and Dow Theory is simply: respect the primary trend, demand confirmation, and don't fight the structure. Every modern tool is a mechanisation of that.

A moving-average trend filter — only hold a stock while it trades above its 200-day average and the 50-day is above the 200-day — is Dow's "trade with the primary trend, assume it persists" tenet written as code. And here the course's honesty rule bites: when we tested exactly that filter across 48 Nifty 50 stocks over 16 years, it beat simple buy-and-hold in only 4 of 48 cases (about 8%), with a median return of +107% versus +744% for just holding. What it did deliver was protection — median worst drawdown of −47% versus −56%, while sitting in cash and out of the market roughly 45% of the time.

Did you know

The 120-year-old idea works exactly as advertised — and exactly as limited. Trading with the primary trend cut the worst drawdown and kept you out during the ugliest stretches, but it badly lagged a simple buy-and-hold in raw return. Dow Theory is a framework for managing risk inside trends, not a machine for beating the market.

That is the right way to carry Dow forward. He did not promise outperformance; he gave you a vocabulary for knowing which way the tide runs and when the structure says it has changed. Use it to control risk and to stop confusing a wave for the tide. The indicators in Module E will just be faster, more precise ways of asking the same questions Dow asked with a pencil and a newspaper.

Quick recap

  • Six tenets: averages discount everything; three trends; three phases; averages confirm each other; volume confirms; a trend holds until clearly reversed.
  • Three nested trends: primary (the tide, months–years), secondary (the wave against it, weeks), minor (daily noise). Always know which one you are reacting to.
  • A trend is structure: higher highs and higher lows for up, lower highs and lower lows for down. The trend changes when a pullback breaks the prior swing low (or high).
  • The cycle runs accumulation → markup → distribution → markdown, each with its own volume signature; you label phases best in hindsight.
  • Volume confirms — it should swell with the primary trend and ebb on counter-moves. Treat divergence as a warning, not a trigger.
  • Tested as a 50/200 trend filter, Dow's logic beat buy-and-hold only ~8% of the time but cut drawdowns — a risk-management framework, not a profit machine.

Next, we take Dow's abstract "trend" and make it concrete: how to actually draw a trendline, decide when it is real, and tell a routine pullback from a genuine change of trend.

The four phases of a primary trend
How Dow Theory shows up today

6.html:
"The trend is your friend" is the most quoted line in trading, and most people stop reading before the rest of it: until the bend at the end. A trend will carry you further than any forecast you could make — right up to the day it quietly stops, usually while you are still congratulating yourself. The skill is not spotting a trend; a child can see a line going up. The skill is knowing, in advance and by rule, the exact point at which you will admit the trend has bent.

This chapter turns Dow's abstract "primary direction" into something you can draw, count and act on. We will define the three trend states precisely, learn to draw a trendline that means something rather than one drawn to flatter a position, and — most importantly — separate an ordinary pullback (buy the dip) from a real change of trend (get out). Get that one distinction right and you are ahead of most chart-watchers.

Three states: up, down, sideways

Everything price does fits into three structures, and we met the definition last chapter:

  • Uptrend — higher highs and higher lows. The buyers keep paying up, and dips keep finding support before the previous low.
  • Downtrend — lower highs and lower lows. Rallies fail earlier each time; declines cut deeper.
  • Sideways (range) — roughly equal highs and lows, price oscillating in a horizontal corridor. Markets spend a surprising amount of their life here, and we give ranges their own chapter next.

The reason this matters is that the same tactic works in opposite directions depending on the state. In an uptrend you buy weakness. In a range you sell strength and buy weakness at the edges. In a downtrend, buying weakness is how accounts die. Step one of any analysis is naming the state — out loud, honestly — before you do anything else.

Drawing a trendline that means something

A trendline is just a straight line connecting the lows in an uptrend or the highs in a downtrend, extended forward as a moving floor or ceiling. The rules for a valid one are simple and worth being strict about:

  • In an uptrend, connect the rising swing lows. The line sits below price and acts as support.
  • In a downtrend, connect the falling swing highs. The line sits above price and acts as resistance.
  • You need at least two points to draw a line, but it takes a third touch to confirm it. Two points are a guess; the third is the market agreeing with your guess.
Heads up

The cardinal sin is forcing the line. If you find yourself cutting through candle bodies, ignoring a touch that doesn't fit, or redrawing the line every few days to keep a losing trade "valid," you are not analysing — you are rationalising. A trendline you had to fight the chart to draw is telling you the trend isn't there.

How many touches make it real? More touches mean more traders see the same line, which makes it more meaningful — and also more obvious, so the eventual break gets crowded. Three to four clean touches is the sweet spot: enough to be real, not so many that the line is exhausted. A line touched eight times is not "stronger"; it is tired, and the next test often breaks it.

One practical wrinkle carries over from the chapter on scales: on a long-running chart, draw your trendlines on a logarithmic scale. On a linear scale, a line that connected the early lows of a multi-year rally will look hopelessly broken halfway up, because equal percentage moves take ever-larger point distances. A 10% move from 100 is 10 points; the same 10% from 400 is 40. A log scale keeps the percentage rise constant, so a trendline that genuinely tracks a percentage-based uptrend stays meaningful for its whole length rather than appearing to "break" simply because the price got bigger.

A real uptrend, measured

Here is what a textbook uptrend looks like in the wild, with no airbrushing.

BEL rose 168.2%, from 174.7 on 12 February 2024 to 468.4 on 6 March 2026. That is the whole point of trends: a single, well-structured primary uptrend did more than any clever in-and-out trading would likely have managed over the same window. But notice what the chart does not show — a straight line. It is a staircase, full of pullbacks that each looked frightening in the moment. Every one of those dips was a "is the trend over?" decision, and every one of them, until the structure actually broke, was a higher low.

from openalgo import ta

# Slope of a moving average is an objective read on trend direction and strength:
# a steadily rising 50-day line under price = an intact, healthy uptrend.
df["sma50"] = ta.sma(df["close"], 50)
df["slope"] = df["sma50"].diff()   # positive and steady = up; flattening = stalling

Trend strength and angle

The angle of a trend tells you about its health, and steeper is not better.

  • A shallow, sustainable slope — price grinding up at a gentle angle, repeatedly reclaiming a rising moving average — is the kind of trend that lasts for months.
  • A near-vertical slope is a warning, not a celebration. Parabolic moves are emotional, run on the final phase of buying, and tend to collapse as fast as they rose. A trendline that has to be redrawn ever-steeper is describing a market running out of buyers, not gaining them.

A useful, objective proxy for strength is the slope of a moving average (above) or simply the spacing of the staircase: are the higher lows still comfortably above the rising average, or is each dip cutting closer to it? Dips that increasingly puncture the average are the trend losing its grip even before the structure formally breaks.

When a trendline breaks — and the pullback trap

This is the part that separates traders from spectators. Not every dip is a reversal, and not every trendline break is the end of the world.

A change of trend is a structural event: in an uptrend, it is the day price closes decisively below the previous higher low, not merely below the drawn line. A trendline can break while the higher-highs/higher-lows structure stays intact — that is often just the trend changing angle, settling into a gentler slope. The swing-low break is the real signal.

A pullback is a secondary move within an intact trend — a dip that holds above the prior higher low and resumes. The whole BEL staircase above was a series of pullbacks that resolved upward. And here the data gives a sober reality check on the popular "buy the dip to the moving average" tactic: across 16 years of Nifty 50 stocks, buying a dip into a rising 50-day average returned +0.67% over the next 10 days, versus a +0.58% baseline for any day in an uptrend. That is a real, positive edge — and a tiny one. The rising average is a sensible place to look for support; it is not a magic floor that pays you for showing up.

Key idea

A trendline break tells you the angle changed. A broken swing low (or swing high) tells you the trend changed. Anchor your exit to the structure — the prior higher low — not to a line you drew by hand. Lines are for attention; structure is for decisions.

Tip

Three honest questions at every pullback: (1) Is the prior higher low still intact? (2) Is volume on the dip light, the way a healthy pullback should be? (3) Is the broader, higher-timeframe trend still up? Three yeses and it is probably a pullback. A no on the first — a broken swing low — and treat it as a change of trend until proven otherwise.

Putting it together

The routine is unglamorous and that is the point. Name the state (up, down, sideways). Draw the line under the lows or over the highs, and refuse to force it. Wait for the third touch before you trust it. Watch the angle for health, not excitement. And when a dip comes — as it always does — judge it against the prior swing point, not against your hopes. A trend really is your friend, but friendship here means following, not predicting. You let the staircase carry you, and you have decided in advance the single stair whose breach ends the relationship.

Quick recap

  • Three states: uptrend (HH/HL), downtrend (LH/LL), sideways (equal highs and lows). Name the state before doing anything.
  • Draw a trendline along rising lows (up) or falling highs (down). Two points to draw, a third touch to confirm. Never force it through the candles.
  • BEL's real uptrend ran +168.2% (174.7 on 12 Feb 2024 → 468.4 on 6 Mar 2026) — a staircase of pullbacks, not a straight line.
  • Angle signals health: shallow and sustained lasts; near-vertical is a warning, not a win.
  • A broken trendline = the angle changed; a broken swing low/high = the trend changed. Anchor decisions to structure.
  • Buying a dip to a rising 50-day average earned +0.67%/10d vs a +0.58% baseline — a tiny edge, not a guaranteed floor.

Next: the horizontal levels where price keeps turning — support and resistance, the zones the crowd remembers until, eventually, they break.

When a trendline breaks — and the pullback trap

7.html:
Markets have a memory, and it is made of people. A price where a crowd of buyers once stepped in, or where a wall of sellers once dumped stock, gets remembered — by the people who traded there, by the people who wished they had, and by the people who got trapped. Months later, when price returns to that same level, those memories turn into orders, and the level holds again. That is the entire mechanism behind support and resistance: not magic lines, but human memory priced into the order book.

The trap is to treat these levels as precise walls that price bounces off forever. They are not. A good level is a zone where the odds of a turn improve, and every single one of them eventually fails — that is how trends are born. This chapter is about marking the levels that genuinely matter, understanding why they form, and respecting the one truth most courses skip: support and resistance exist to be tested, retested, and finally broken.

What support and resistance actually are

  • Support is a price zone where buying has previously been strong enough to halt a decline. Demand overwhelms supply, and price bounces.
  • Resistance is a price zone where selling has previously been strong enough to halt an advance. Supply overwhelms demand, and price stalls.

That is it. Support is a floor where buyers keep showing up; resistance is a ceiling where sellers keep showing up. Both are simply memory of where supply and demand last fought, and the more often and more recently they fought there, the more the level matters.

Key idea

Support and resistance are not about the line being "strong." They are about a price level where enough traders share the same memory that their orders cluster there. The level holds because people act on the memory — until enough of them give up or get overrun.

The psychology behind a level

Why do orders cluster at a remembered price? Three groups of people, all looking at the same number:

  • The ones who bought there and watched it work. When price comes back to that level, they buy more — it "worked last time."
  • The ones who missed it. They watched price leave without them and swore they would buy if it ever came back. Now it has.
  • The ones who got trapped. They bought too high, sat through a loss, and just want to get out "at breakeven." When price returns to their entry, they sell — creating resistance exactly where they bought.

That third group is the engine of role reversal, the most useful idea in the chapter. Notice that all three groups are reacting to the same level for different reasons, and their combined orders are what make the bounce or rejection happen. The level is self-fulfilling precisely because everyone can see it.

Round numbers and prior highs and lows

Where do these levels come from? A few reliable sources:

  • Prior swing highs and lows. The most important. A previous peak becomes resistance; a previous trough becomes support. These are the levels the structure itself remembers.
  • Round numbers. 100, 500, 1000, 1500. Humans place orders at round figures — stops, targets, alerts all cluster there — so price often reacts at psychologically "big" numbers even with no prior history.
  • Prior consolidation zones. A region where price spent a lot of time tends to act as a magnet and a barrier later; lots of volume changed hands there, so lots of memories live there.
Tip

The best levels are the ones marked by both a structural reason and a round number — a prior swing high that also sits near 1500, say. When two independent reasons point to the same zone, more traders see it, more orders cluster, and the level carries more weight. We will call that overlap "confluence" again in the Fibonacci chapter.

Role reversal: the floor becomes the ceiling

The single most practical behaviour of these levels is that once broken, they swap roles. Broken resistance becomes support; broken support becomes resistance.

The mechanism is the trapped-trader story again. While a level is resistance, sellers who bought below are happy and the rally stalls. Once price breaks above and holds, those earlier buyers are now in profit, the doubters who sold are now wrong, and a fresh group wants to buy any dip back to the breakout point. So the old ceiling becomes the new floor. This is why a retest of a broken level — price coming back to touch it from the other side — is one of the cleaner, lower-risk entries in technical analysis: you are buying at a level the market has just demonstrated it will defend.

Draw zones, not lines

Beginners draw support and resistance as a single, pixel-perfect line and then feel betrayed when price overshoots it by a few rupees. Professionals draw zones — a band a little wider than a single price — because that is how levels actually behave.

A level is a region of agreement, not a number. Wicks poke through it, closes pull back inside it, and different traders remember slightly different prices. Mark the zone from the cluster of highs or lows, not the single most extreme tick. A useful, mechanical starting point is to let a recent swing high and low define a band and let price tell you which edges matter.

from openalgo import ta

# A rough, objective support/resistance band from the recent range -
# the highest high and lowest low of the last 20 sessions. Treat the
# edges as zones to watch, not exact lines to trade blindly.
df["res_zone"] = ta.highest(df["high"], 20)
df["sup_zone"] = ta.lowest(df["low"], 20)

Why levels eventually fail — tested and broken

Here is the honest part nobody frames properly: a level's whole job is to be broken eventually. Support and resistance are not permanent walls; they are pressure points that hold until the supply/demand balance changes, and then they give way. Watching how many times a level is tested before it breaks tells you more than the level itself.

On this RELIANCE chart, resistance near 1577 was tested on 14 separate sessions, and support near 1291 on 13 sessions. Read that carefully: those are not 14 successful "bounces" proving the level is invincible. They are 14 attacks. Each retest chips away at the supply sitting there, absorbing the sellers, until one day the orders are exhausted and price breaks through. A level tested over and over is not getting stronger — it is being worn down, and a much-tested ceiling that finally cracks often releases a sharp move because so much pent-up demand was sitting just above it.

Heads up

Do not confuse "tested many times" with "will hold forever." Repeated tests are a countdown, not a guarantee. The more often price grinds against a level, the more you should prepare for the break — and the breakout, not the bounce, is frequently the bigger trade. The next chapter shows why even that breakout fools you most of the time.

Did you know

Levels are zones, not lines, and they are temporary. RELIANCE's resistance at ~1577 (14 tests) and support at ~1291 (13 tests) held repeatedly — until they didn't. That is the normal life cycle of every support and resistance level: form, get tested, get retested, and eventually give way to a new trend.

Putting it together

Mark the zones where price has a memory — prior swings, round numbers, busy consolidations — and draw them as bands, not razor lines. Expect broken levels to flip roles, and treat the retest as your higher-quality entry. Above all, hold the two ideas at once: a level improves your odds of a turn, and it will eventually fail. The trader who respects the level but is mentally prepared for its break is the one who neither fights the bounce nor gets blindsided by the breakout.

Quick recap

  • Support = a floor where buyers repeatedly step in; resistance = a ceiling where sellers repeatedly step in. Both are crowd memory of where supply and demand last fought.
  • The psychology is three groups acting on one price: those who bought there, those who missed it, and the trapped traders waiting to get out at breakeven.
  • Role reversal: broken resistance becomes support and vice versa; the retest of a broken level is a cleaner, lower-risk entry.
  • Levels come from prior swing highs/lows, round numbers, and old consolidation zones — strongest where two reasons overlap (confluence).
  • Draw zones, not lines — a band around the cluster of highs or lows, because wicks overshoot and traders remember slightly different prices.
  • Every level eventually fails by design: RELIANCE's ~1577 resistance was tested on 14 sessions and ~1291 support on 13 before giving way. Repeated tests are a countdown, not a fortress.

Next: what happens after a level breaks — channels, ranges, and the breakout that fools traders more than half the time.
