Double and triple tops and bottoms
The neckline is the whole pattern
False reversals and the pattern-spotting trap

14.html:
A strong trend does not move in a straight line. It surges, then pauses; surges, then pauses again. Those pauses are where most traders get shaken out — the trend looks like it is rolling over, profit-takers cash in, the chart goes quiet — right before it picks up where it left off. Continuation patterns are the shapes those pauses make, and learning to read them is mostly about learning not to mistake a breather for a reversal.

The previous chapter was about trends ending. This one is about trends resting. The same sideways squeeze that a nervous trader reads as "the top is in" is often just the market catching its breath. The skill is telling the two apart — and the honest news is you usually cannot, not with certainty, until price breaks out of the pause and resolves the question for you. So we will treat these patterns the same way we treated reversals: as setups that must confirm, with a measured target to judge the reward and a defined level that says we were wrong.

What a continuation pattern is

A continuation pattern is a consolidation that interrupts a trend without reversing it. Price has run, it needs to digest the move, so buyers and sellers fight to a temporary draw inside a tidy range, triangle or channel. When the pattern resolves, it usually resolves in the direction of the trend that preceded it — the pause ends and the move continues. The key word is usually: the same shapes can, and sometimes do, break the other way and become reversals. The pattern does not decide; the breakout does.

Key idea

The defining feature of a continuation pattern is the trend before it. A flag after a sharp rally is a continuation candidate. The identical flag drawn on a directionless chart is just a range. Always read the pattern in the context of the move that led into it.

Flags and pennants

Flags and pennants are the short, sharp pauses that follow an almost vertical move — the "flagpole". A flag is a small rectangle that drifts gently against the trend (a bull flag slopes slightly down, a bear flag slightly up). A pennant is a small symmetrical triangle that contracts to a point. Both are brief — typically a handful of sessions to a few weeks — and both represent a quick rest after a strong thrust before the trend resumes.

The reason they tilt against the trend matters: after a hard rally, weak hands take profits and the pullback is shallow and orderly, because dip-buyers keep stepping in. That shallow, controlled drift is the signature of a pause rather than a top. A flag that turns into a deep, accelerating sell-off is no longer a flag — it is telling you the move is over.

Triangles: ascending, descending, symmetrical

Triangles are longer consolidations where the range narrows as buyers and sellers converge. There are three:

Triangle Shape Typical lean
Ascending Flat top, rising lows Buyers getting more aggressive; often breaks up
Descending Flat bottom, falling highs Sellers getting more aggressive; often breaks down
Symmetrical Lower highs and higher lows Neutral coil; breaks in the trend's direction more often than not

An ascending triangle has a flat resistance line being hit repeatedly while the lows keep rising — buyers willing to pay up sooner each time, pressing price against a ceiling until it gives. A descending triangle is the mirror, with sellers pressing a floor. A symmetrical triangle coils between converging lines and carries the least directional bias on its own — which is exactly why the prior trend is your best guide to which way it is likely to break.

Heads up

Do not assume the lean. An ascending triangle "should" break up and a descending one "should" break down — but plenty break the other way, and a descending triangle inside a strong uptrend frequently resolves upward. The shape suggests a bias; only the actual break confirms it. Trade the break, not the prediction.

Rectangles and consolidations

A rectangle is a flat range — price bouncing between horizontal support and resistance — that appears within a trend rather than at its end. It is the market trading in a holding pattern, with neither side able to push through. In a continuation context, a rectangle in an uptrend tends to break upward as the trend reasserts. But a rectangle is also the most ambiguous of these shapes: the very same flat range is what a reversal (like a double top) looks like while it is forming. Once again, the resolution — which boundary breaks — is what names the pattern.

Measuring the move after a break

Like reversals, continuation patterns come with a geometric target. For flags and pennants, the classic method measures the flagpole — the height of the sharp move that preceded the pattern — and projects that same distance from the breakout point. The idea is that the move "continues" by roughly as much as it had already run. For triangles and rectangles, you measure the height of the pattern at its widest point and project it from the break.

from openalgo import ta

# A continuation pause shows up as falling volatility before the break.
# ATR (average true range) contracting through the pattern is a useful tell.
df["atr"] = ta.atr(df["high"], df["low"], df["close"], 14)
Tip

A measured move is a reward estimate, nothing more. Pair it with the level that would prove you wrong — usually the far side of the pattern — to get a reward-to-risk ratio before you enter. If the projected target is barely larger than the risk to your stop, the cleanest-looking pattern in the world is not worth taking.

There is a deeper reason these targets are only estimates. A continuation pattern is the market reaching a temporary balance, and balance can resolve in either direction or simply fizzle into a longer range. The flagpole projection assumes the second half of the move mirrors the first; sometimes it overshoots, often it falls short, and occasionally the breakout fails outright and the "second half" never arrives. Treat the number as a way to decide whether the trade is worth taking, not as a price the market owes you. The discipline is in the entry, the stop and the position size — the target just helps you judge if the maths is favourable before you commit.

Continuation versus reversal

This is the question the whole chapter circles around, and it deserves an honest answer: you often cannot know in advance. A symmetrical triangle, a rectangle, even a flag can resolve either way. What tilts the odds toward continuation is straightforward and worth a checklist:

  • A strong, clear trend leading into the pattern.
  • A pause that is proportionate — a shallow flag, a tidy coil — not a deep, violent reversal of character.
  • Volume that dries up through the consolidation and expands on the breakout in the trend's direction.
  • A break that resolves on the same side as the prior trend.

When those align, you have a continuation setup. When the "pause" is deep, volume swells against the trend, and price breaks the opposite way, you are most likely watching a reversal — and the right response is to respect it, not to argue with the chart.

Where these patterns fail

A breakout from a flag or triangle is still just a breakout, and breakouts fail at uncomfortable rates. Measured across the Nifty 50, 56% of 20-day breakouts fell back below the breakout level within five days — more often a fakeout than a clean run. Filtering for volume helped but did not rescue it: breakouts on more than 1.5x average volume failed 47% of the time, with better follow-through on the ones that worked. So volume confirmation tilts the odds and improves the survivors, but roughly half still fail either way.

Did you know

"Patterns are reliable" is the comforting half-truth this course keeps puncturing. Even a textbook continuation pattern that breaks the right way has, historically, been close to a coin flip on follow-through. The edge does not live in spotting the shape — it lives in waiting for the break, demanding volume, sizing for a stop, and accepting that a large share will fail. Risk control, not pattern recognition, is what keeps you in the game.

Trading them without fooling yourself

The practical routine mirrors the reversal chapter. Identify the trend first. Mark the pattern's boundaries before the break, not after. Wait for a decisive close beyond the boundary, ideally on expanding volume. Use the measured move as your reward and the far side of the pattern as your stop, and only take the trade if the two make sense together. Expect the retest — price frequently throws back to the broken boundary before continuing, and that retest is often a lower-risk entry than chasing the first candle. And keep your humility intact: about half of even good-looking breaks do not follow through, so no single pattern deserves an outsized bet.

Quick recap

  • Continuation patterns are pauses in a trend, not turns: flags, pennants, ascending/descending/symmetrical triangles, and rectangles.
  • The trend before the pattern is what makes it a continuation candidate; the breakout direction is what confirms it.
  • Measure the flagpole (flags/pennants) or the pattern height (triangles/rectangles) and project it from the break to set a target — a reward estimate, not a promise.
  • Volume should dry up through the pause and expand on the break; a break against the trend on swelling volume points to reversal instead.
  • Breakouts fail often (56% of 20-day breakouts snapped back within five days; 47% even on high volume), so confirm, expect a retest, and size for the stop.

Next: sometimes price does not consolidate at all — it leaps. We turn to gaps, the jumps that open well away from yesterday's close, and what each kind really tells you.

toc.text: Measuring the move after a break

15.html:
A stock closes at 1,200 on Tuesday and opens at 1,290 on Wednesday. The chart shows it — a visible blank space, a gap, where no trading happened. Price did not walk from one level to the next; it jumped the queue. Something happened overnight, while the market was shut, that everyone agreed mattered: a results announcement, a regulatory order, a global selloff, a takeover rumour. By the time the bell rang, the new information was already in the price, and the only way for the market to express it was to open somewhere else entirely.

Gaps are among the most misunderstood things on a chart, weighed down by one stubborn piece of folklore — "all gaps get filled" — that the data flatly contradicts. Some gaps do fill quickly and mean almost nothing. Others mark the explosive start of a move that never looks back. The whole skill is telling those apart, and the honest answer is that the size of the gap and the context it opens in tell you far more than any blanket rule. Let us take them in order.

Why gaps form

A gap is simply a session opening away from the previous close, leaving a price range that never traded. They form because markets are closed for far more hours than they are open, and information does not wait. Most Indian gaps you will care about come from scheduled, high-impact events: quarterly results, management guidance, dividend or bonus news, an order win or loss, a credit-rating change, or an overnight move in global markets and commodities that repositions the whole index at the open. The bigger and more surprising the news, the bigger the gap.

Key idea

A gap is the market repricing in one step instead of many. The questions to ask are always the same: what news caused it, how big is the jump, where in the trend did it happen, and did volume confirm it? Those four answers — not a rule of thumb — tell you which kind of gap you are looking at.

The four kinds of gap

Gaps are traditionally sorted into four types by where they appear and what they do next. The labels are descriptive, not predictive — you often only know for certain which one you had in hindsight — but they are a genuinely useful framework for thinking about what a gap means.

  • Common gaps happen inside a range or a quiet drift, on ordinary news or none at all. They are small, carry little meaning, and tend to fill quickly as price wanders back across them. The bulk of everyday gaps are these.
  • Breakaway gaps launch a new move — price gaps out of a consolidation, a base or a chart pattern, often on heavy volume and real news. These are the gaps that matter most, because they frequently do not fill: the gap itself is the start of a trend, and filling it would undo the breakout.
  • Runaway gaps (also called measuring gaps) appear in the middle of an established trend, as a fresh wave of buyers or sellers piles in and accelerates a move already underway. They often sit roughly halfway through the total move, which is where the "measuring" nickname comes from.
  • Exhaustion gaps come near the end of an extended move — a last, frantic leap as the final latecomers chase. They look powerful but tend to stall and reverse soon after, and they usually do fill. An exhaustion gap and a breakaway gap can look identical on the day; what separates them is the trend's age and what price does in the sessions that follow.

Occasionally these combine into a striking shape called an island reversal: price gaps up on exhaustion, trades sideways for a session or a few at the new high, then gaps back down — leaving a small cluster of bars stranded above the rest of the chart like an island, with empty gaps on both sides. It is a vivid sign that the late buyers who chased the first gap are now trapped and selling. The mirror version at a bottom marks a sharp turn up. Islands are uncommon, but when one forms after a long trend it is one of the more reliable warnings that the move has flipped.

Do gaps get filled?

Here is where folklore meets evidence. "Gaps always fill" is comforting because it implies an easy trade — short the gap-up, buy the gap-down, wait for the magnet. The data says be careful. Measured across the Nifty 50, gaps fill often but not always, and crucially, the bigger the gap, the less likely it is to fill:

Did you know

On real Nifty 50 daily data, gaps over 2% filled within 20 sessions about 78-79% of the time — high, but not the "always" of the myth. And gaps over 5% filled only about 71-73% of the time. That is the opposite of what folklore suggests: the largest gaps, the dramatic ones you most want to fade, are the least likely to come back. Big gaps tend to be breakaway gaps, and breakaway gaps run.

The lesson is not "gaps never fill" — most do, eventually. It is that fading a gap is a probability bet that gets worse exactly as the gap gets more exciting. A small gap inside a range is a reasonable fill candidate; a 6% gap out of a long base on huge volume and a fresh earnings beat is the last thing you want to short on the assumption it must come back.

Gaps with volume

Volume is the single best tell for separating a meaningful gap from a throwaway one. A gap on heavy volume says real money repositioned on the news — it has conviction behind it and is more likely to hold and extend (the breakaway and runaway cases). A gap on light volume is thinner, more likely a common gap, and more likely to drift back and fill. The same logic flags exhaustion: when a gap comes after a long trend on a climactic volume spike but then fails to make further progress, the market is showing you a buying or selling climax — the last of the demand or supply being spent.

from openalgo import ta

# A gap is just today's open versus yesterday's close, in percent.
df["prev_close"] = df["close"].shift(1)
df["gap_pct"] = (df["open"] - df["prev_close"]) / df["prev_close"] * 100

# Pair it with volume relative to its recent average to judge conviction.
df["vol_avg20"] = ta.sma(df["volume"], 20)
df["vol_ratio"] = df["volume"] / df["vol_avg20"]
Tip

Read a gap as two numbers together: how big it is, and how much volume came with it. A large gap on big volume out of a base is a breakaway to respect, not fade. A small gap on quiet volume inside a range is the kind that usually fills. The size tells you the conviction; the volume confirms it.

Trading around a gap safely

Gaps are dangerous precisely because they are exciting — they pull traders into chasing or fading on impulse, at the worst possible moment of the day. A few honest guidelines keep you out of the obvious traps.

First, do not chase the open. The opening minutes after a big gap are the most volatile and least informative of the session; price is still discovering where the new fair value sits. Letting the first range form before acting costs you nothing and saves you from the immediate reversal.

Second, respect the gap level itself. The previous close, and the open of the gap, become meaningful support or resistance — much like the role-reversal levels from earlier in the course. A gap-up that holds above its prior close is showing strength; one that slips back below it is failing, and that failure is itself information.

Heads up

The riskiest trade on a gap day is fading a large gap on the assumption it "must fill". You are betting against the news, against volume, and against the data — remember that gaps over 5% filled only about 71-73% of the time, so you are on the wrong side of the base rate on the very gaps most likely to keep running. If you trade a gap at all, trade in the direction the volume confirms, and define your stop before you enter.

Third, size down. Gaps mean volatility, and volatility means a wider sensible stop, which means a smaller position to keep your risk constant. The arithmetic of risk does not change because the chart looks thrilling — if anything, a gap day is when discipline matters most.

Quick recap

  • A gap is the market repricing in one jump on overnight news (results, ratings, global moves) — a price range that never traded.
  • Four kinds: common (noise, fills fast), breakaway (starts a trend, often does not fill), runaway/measuring (mid-trend acceleration), exhaustion (end of a move, usually fills then reverses).
  • "All gaps fill" is a myth: Nifty 50 gaps over 2% filled ~78-79% within 20 sessions, but gaps over 5% filled only ~71-73% — bigger gaps run.
  • Volume separates a meaningful gap from a throwaway one; heavy volume = conviction, light volume = likely fill.
  • Trade gaps with care: don't chase the open, respect the gap level, never fade a big gap just because folklore says it must fill, and size down for the volatility.

Next, we leave chart patterns behind and turn to the math laid on top of price — starting with moving averages, the trend smoothed into a single line, and the golden and death crosses, measured against their real base rates.

16.html:
Raw price is a jittery thing. A stock can close up, down, up, down across a week and leave you squinting at the candles, unsure whether the trend is your friend or your trap. A moving average answers one honest question: ignoring the day-to-day noise, which way has price been leaning lately? It does that by taking the average close of the last N days and re-drawing it every day, so the line moves forward with price. The jagged becomes smooth, and a direction appears.

That smoothing is the whole appeal — and the whole catch. A line built from the past can only ever describe the past a little late. The most famous moving-average signals, the "golden cross" and the "death cross," are sold as buy and sell buttons. When we measured them on 16 years of Nifty 50 data, the buttons were wired backwards from what the textbooks promise. Let us build the tool properly first, then look at what it actually delivers.

Two ways to average: SMA vs EMA

A Simple Moving Average (SMA) adds up the last N closes and divides by N. Every day in the window counts equally — the close from 50 days ago matters exactly as much as yesterday's. It is calm and steady, which is also its flaw: a sharp turn today is diluted by 49 older days.

An Exponential Moving Average (EMA) fixes the lag by weighting recent days more heavily. Yesterday's close moves the line more than the close from a month ago, so the EMA hugs price tighter and turns sooner. The cost is that it also reacts to noise sooner — more responsiveness, more false twitches. Neither is "better"; the EMA trades lag for jumpiness, the SMA trades jumpiness for lag.

from openalgo import ta

df["sma50"]  = ta.sma(df["close"], 50)
df["ema50"]  = ta.ema(df["close"], 50)
df["sma200"] = ta.sma(df["close"], 200)
Key idea

A moving average is a follower, never a leader. It can only tell you what price has already done, smoothed. Treat it as a description of the trend's direction and slope — not a prediction of the next bar.

The lengths everyone watches: 20, 50, 200

Three windows have become a shared language simply because so many traders watch them:

  • 20-day — roughly a trading month. A fast line for short-term swing direction.
  • 50-day — about a quarter. The classic "intermediate trend" gauge.
  • 200-day — close to a trading year. The line institutions cite when they call a stock "above its 200-DMA" — shorthand for a healthy long-term trend.

There is nothing magic in these numbers; they work partly because the crowd watches them, which makes them self-fulfilling levels of attention. A 50-day average on a daily chart and a 50-day average on a weekly chart describe completely different trends, so always know which timeframe you are on.

A line you can lean on — sometimes

In a clean uptrend, price often pulls back to a rising moving average and bounces, as if the line were a moving floor. This is the "dynamic support" idea, and it has a small real edge. Across the Nifty 50, when an uptrending stock dipped to its rising 50-day average, the next 10 days returned +0.67% on average — versus +0.58% for any day in an uptrend. That is an edge of less than a tenth of a percent.

Heads up

The rising-average "floor" is a tendency, not a wall. The +0.67% vs +0.58% gap is real but tiny — about a rounding error. Price slices straight through these averages all the time. A moving average is a zone of interest, not a guaranteed bounce, and never a place to skip your stop-loss.

Slope is the real message

Before any crossover, read the slope. A moving average angling up means the average buyer of the last N days is in profit and the trend leans up; a flattening average means the trend is stalling; a rolling-over average means control is shifting. The angle tells you more than the exact price touch. A stock can sit above its 200-day average while that average flattens — momentum draining even though price is still technically "above the line."

The golden cross and the death cross

The headline signals are crossovers of two averages. A golden cross is the 50-day average crossing above the 200-day — supposedly bullish. A death cross is the 50-day crossing below the 200-day — supposedly bearish. The names alone tell you which one financial media gets excited about.

Here is a textbook-perfect golden cross. BEL's 50-day average crossed above its 200-day on 12 May 2025 at 322.8, and the stock gained 19.8% over the next 60 sessions. Exactly what the story promises.

And here is a death cross doing its job. TCS's 50-day average crossed below its 200-day on 17 February 2025 at 3904, and the stock fell 9.7% over the next 60 sessions.

Two clean wins for the textbook. But two charts are an anecdote, not evidence. So we measured every cross across the Nifty 50 — 527 golden crosses and 529 death crosses over 16 years — and the result inverts the folklore.

The golden cross was positive only 59% of the time at 60 days, with a median gain of just +2.7% — and the typical winning path still made you sit through a −7.3% drawdown before it worked. Stretch to 120 days and the median is +6.4%, which is actually below the roughly +9% you would have earned simply holding through the same period. The signal lags so badly that by the time the 50-day has crawled above the 200-day, much of the move is already gone.

Now the punchline. The death cross — the one you are told means "sell" — was positive 66% of the time at 60 days, with a median gain of +4.8%. The supposedly bearish signal was more often up, and rose more, than the supposedly bullish one. Why? A death cross usually fires after a long decline, often near a washed-out, oversold low — precisely when the next bounce is brewing.

Did you know

On the Nifty 50, the death cross beat the golden cross on both how-often-up (66% vs 59% at 60 days) and median return (+4.8% vs +2.7%). "Golden cross = buy, death cross = sell" is not a rule — on this data it is closer to backwards. The cross tells you a trend changed recently; it does not tell you what happens next.

The baseline matters here. The average Nifty 50 day drifts +1.48% over the next 20 days — roughly +4.5% over 60. Measured against that drift, the golden cross's +2.7%/60d is barely an improvement, while the cost of acting on it (the lag, the −7.3% drawdown) is very real.

When the line turns too often

The deeper problem with any trend line is whipsaw: in choppy, sideways markets the averages cross back and forth, handing you a string of small losses. Adaptive trend tools like Supertrend — which trails price using volatility (ATR) rather than a fixed average — try to react faster, but they pay the same tax. On BEL, the Supertrend flipped direction 25 times in two years. Most of those flips were noise, not trend changes.

from openalgo import ta
# A volatility-based trailing trend line (ATR-based)
supertrend, direction = ta.supertrend(df["high"], df["low"], df["close"], 10, 3)

This is the lag trade-off stated plainly. Slow averages (the 200-day, a wide SMA) give few signals, late — you keep most of a big trend but give back a chunk at every turn. Fast averages (a short EMA, a tight Supertrend) give many signals, early — you catch turns sooner but get chopped to pieces when there is no trend. There is no setting that removes the trade-off; you can only choose which side of it to pay.

Tip

Use moving averages for what they are genuinely good at: reading the direction and slope of the trend, and framing context ("price is above a rising 50-day, so I favour long setups"). Do not use a single crossover as a standalone buy or sell trigger — the base rates say it barely beats just being invested, and often lags it.

Real example

A practical, honest way to use the 50/200 relationship is as a filter, not a trigger: only hunt for long entries while the 50-day is above a rising 200-day, and stand aside when it is not. As a later chapter's backtest will show, this kind of filter does not make more money than buy-and-hold — it usually makes less — but it can meaningfully cut your drawdown and time in the market. That is a risk decision, not a profit machine.

Quick recap

  • A moving average smooths price into a trend line; SMA weights all days equally (calmer, slower), EMA weights recent days more (faster, jumpier).
  • The watched lengths — 20, 50, 200 — matter largely because the crowd watches them. Always know the timeframe.
  • A rising average acts as loose dynamic support (+0.67% vs +0.58% baseline on a 50-DMA dip) — a tendency, never a guaranteed floor.
  • Slope beats the exact touch: a flattening average warns of a stalling trend before any cross.
  • The golden cross was positive just 59% at 60 days (+2.7% median, after a −7.3% drawdown) and lagged buy-and-hold by 120 days; the death cross was more often up (66%, +4.8%). "Golden = buy, death = sell" does not survive the data.
  • Every trend tool pays the lag trade-off: slow = late but smooth, fast = early but whipsawed (BEL's Supertrend flipped 25 times in two years).

Moving averages smooth the trend but tell you little about momentum — how forcefully price is moving right now. Next we put a number on that pulse with the RSI, and discover that "overbought" does not mean what you have been told.
