When the line turns too often

17.html:
Momentum is the speed of a trend, and the Relative Strength Index is the most popular way to put a number on it. RSI takes the recent up-closes and down-closes, weighs them against each other, and squeezes the result onto a 0–100 scale. Near 100, buyers have utterly dominated the lookback window; near 0, sellers have. It is a clean, bounded reading of how forceful a move has been.

Every chart platform draws the same two dashed lines on the RSI panel: 70 and 30. And almost every beginner is taught the same false sentence — "above 70, sell; below 30, buy." It is one of the most repeated rules in trading, and on 16 years of Nifty 50 data it is simply wrong. Not "needs nuance" wrong — backwards wrong. The most overbought readings produced the best forward returns. Let us see why.

What RSI actually measures

RSI compares the average gain on up-days to the average loss on down-days over a lookback period — 14 days by default. The ratio of those two averages is called Relative Strength (RS), and RSI rescales it with the formula 100 − 100 / (1 + RS) so the output is always pinned between 0 and 100. If gains and losses are balanced, RS is 1 and RSI sits near 50. As up-days dominate, it climbs toward 100; as down-days dominate, it sinks toward 0. Because the formula is bounded, RSI — unlike price — can never run off the top of the chart, which is exactly what makes it comparable across a ₹200 stock and a ₹4,000 stock on the same scale.

Note that "Relative Strength" here is internal: RSI measures a stock against its own recent history, not against an index or another stock. That is a common confusion worth clearing early — RSI is a self-referential momentum gauge, nothing to do with comparative strength versus the Nifty.

from openalgo import ta
df["rsi"] = ta.rsi(df["close"], 14)

The conventional labels: above 70 is "overbought," below 30 is "oversold." Those words are accurate as descriptions — at RSI 75 the stock genuinely has been bought hard. The error is treating the description as an instruction.

Key idea

"Overbought" means strong, not about to fall. "Oversold" means weak, not about to bounce. RSI measures the momentum that already happened; it does not contain a timer for when that momentum will end.

The number that debunks the rule

We bucketed every Nifty 50 day by its RSI(14) reading and measured the average return over the next 20 days. The baseline — the drift of any random Nifty 50 day — is +1.48% over 20 days. Here is what each bucket actually delivered:

RSI(14) bucket Avg forward 20-day return
Below 30 ("oversold") +1.8%
50 – 70 (normal) +1.4%
70 – 80 ("overbought") +1.4%
Above 80 (extreme) +2.2% (60% positive — the best)

Read that bottom row again. The most overbought stocks — RSI above 80, the deepest "sell" zone in the textbook — went on to the highest forward return of any bucket, and were positive 60% of the time. The oversold (<30) bucket did fine too at +1.8%, but the "obvious sell" at the top was the best place to be long, not short.

The reason is mechanical. A stock reaches RSI 80+ precisely because it is in a powerful uptrend, and strong trends tend to keep trending. Selling strength because a momentum gauge is high means betting against the very force the gauge is measuring.

Did you know

On the Nifty 50, the RSI > 80 bucket — the "extremely overbought, definitely sell" zone — had the single best forward 20-day return of every bucket (+2.2%, 60% positive). The cliché "70 = sell, 30 = buy" does not survive contact with the data.

Overbought can stay overbought

The other half of the myth is timing. Even traders who know not to short strength assume "overbought" is a fleeting, one-day flag. It is not. On this data, RSI-above-70 streaks ran as long as 43 consecutive days, and RSI-below-30 streaks as long as 44 days. A reading is a state a stock can live in for weeks, not a same-day switch to flip.

So a stock can print RSI 75 and keep climbing for a month, while you wait for the "sell signal" to pay off. And oversold cuts the same way. Here is Asian Paints driving the point home: on 25 January 2024 its RSI hit 8.7 — about as oversold as a major stock ever gets — and the textbook screamed "buy." Over the next 20 days the stock managed only +1.2%, below the +1.48% baseline, and then kept grinding lower. In that single stock, RSI dipped below 30 on 76 separate sessions; "oversold" was not a rare bottom-fishing signal, it was the stock's normal condition during a downtrend.

Heads up

A deeply oversold RSI in a downtrend is not a bargain — it is confirmation the trend is strong, to the downside. Buying it because "30 = buy" is catching a falling knife. RSI extremes need the trend's context before they mean anything.

Where RSI earns its keep: divergence and context

If the threshold rule is broken, what is RSI good for? Two things.

Divergence. This is RSI's most respected use. A bearish divergence is when price makes a higher high but RSI makes a lower high — the new price peak was achieved with less momentum than the last, a hint the trend is tiring. A bullish divergence is the mirror: price makes a lower low while RSI makes a higher low, suggesting selling pressure is fading even as price slips. The logic is intuitive: price set a new extreme, but it took less underlying force to get there, so the engine driving the move is quietly losing power.

Two honest cautions about divergence. First, it can persist for a long time before price actually turns — a strong trend can throw off divergence signals for weeks while it keeps running, so divergence alone is a notoriously early and unreliable entry. Second, divergence is best read at the extremes of a move, near established support or resistance, not in the middle of a trend where it means little. Used well, divergence does not name a date; it is an early warning that the force behind a move is weakening, best paired with a price trigger — a broken trendline, a reversal candle — before you act.

Trend vs range. RSI behaves completely differently depending on the regime:

  • In a range, price oscillates between support and resistance, and RSI swings obediently between its extremes — here the 70/30 levels are more useful as "the band is stretched" markers (still not standalone triggers).
  • In a trend, RSI gets pinned. An uptrend keeps RSI riding the 60–80 zone for weeks; a downtrend pins it 20–40. Applying range rules to a trending stock is exactly how the "sell at 70" mistake bankrupts a momentum trade.

Some momentum traders even flip the thresholds in a strong trend: in a confirmed uptrend they treat RSI holding above 40 as the sign of health, and a break below 40 as the warning, ignoring the 70 line entirely. You do not have to adopt that exact rule, but it captures the right instinct — the meaning of any RSI level depends entirely on the regime it is printing in.

Tip

Read RSI as context, never as a trigger. In a confirmed uptrend, an RSI dip toward 40–50 (not 30) often marks the pause before continuation — far more useful than waiting for a "30" that a strong stock may never print. Let price and structure give the entry; let RSI describe the momentum behind it.

Tuning the lookback

The default is 14, but the lookback is a dial. A shorter RSI (say 7) reacts faster and hits extremes more often — more signals, more noise. A longer RSI (say 21) is slower and smoother — fewer, more deliberate swings. Shortening the period is the same trade-off you met with moving averages: more responsiveness bought with more false alarms. Most traders leave it at 14 not because 14 is optimal, but because it is the shared default everyone else is reacting to. Whatever you choose, the interpretation rules above do not change — the thresholds still describe a state, not an order to act.

Real example

A workable RSI routine: first decide the trend from price and a moving average. If the trend is up, ignore overbought readings and instead watch for shallow RSI dips and bullish divergence as continuation cues. If the trend is sideways, treat stretched RSI as a "this leg is extended" caution near the range edges. Either way, RSI confirms; it never leads.

Quick recap

  • RSI puts momentum on a 0–100 scale from average up-closes vs down-closes over a lookback (default 14).
  • "70 = sell, 30 = buy" is false on this data: the RSI > 80 bucket had the best forward 20-day return (+2.2%, 60% positive); oversold (<30) returned +1.8%.
  • Overbought and oversold are states, not switches — streaks ran up to 43 (overbought) and 44 (oversold) days; Asian Paints hit RSI 8.7 and still fell.
  • RSI's real value is divergence (momentum fading against price) and regime context (it rides high in uptrends, low in downtrends).
  • The lookback trades responsiveness for noise; the interpretation never changes — RSI describes momentum, it does not predict the turn.

RSI reads momentum but says nothing about the trend it is riding. The MACD tries to do both at once — blending two moving averages into a single momentum-and-trend tool. Next we build it, read its crossover, and measure how often that famous signal is worth acting on.

toc.text: The number that debunks the rule
Where RSI earns its keep: divergence and context

18.html:
Moving averages tell you the trend. RSI tells you the momentum. The MACD — Moving Average Convergence Divergence — is the tool that tries to fold both into a single panel, which is exactly why it became one of the most-watched indicators on every trading screen. It does this with no new mathematics at all: just two moving averages and the gap between them.

That gap is the whole idea. When a fast average pulls away from a slow one, the trend is accelerating; when they converge, momentum is fading; when they cross, something has shifted. The MACD turns that distance into a wiggling line, a signal line, and a histogram — and traders treat the crossover of those lines as a buy or sell trigger. As with the golden cross, the data has an opinion about that, and it is a sobering one: across the Nifty 50, the famous MACD bullish crossover beat a modest +5% only 37% of the time. Let us build it first, then look honestly at what the cross is worth.

How MACD is built

Three pieces, stacked from two EMAs:

  1. MACD line = the 12-day EMA minus the 26-day EMA. When the fast (12) average is above the slow (26), this line is positive; when below, negative. It measures how far the short-term trend has separated from the medium-term one.
  2. Signal line = a 9-day EMA of the MACD line. A smoothed, slower version of the MACD line itself.
  3. Histogram = the MACD line minus the signal line, drawn as bars. It visualises the gap between the two lines — the rate at which momentum is building or fading.
from openalgo import ta
macd_line, signal_line, histogram = ta.macd(df["close"], 12, 26, 9)

The 12/26/9 settings are the defaults everyone shares; like the RSI's 14, their power is partly that the crowd watches the same numbers. You can tune them — faster settings (say 5/13/5) react sooner but whipsaw more, slower settings smooth out noise at the cost of even more lag — but the same trade-off you met with moving averages applies, and most traders leave the defaults precisely so they are reading the same picture as everyone else. Because MACD is built from EMAs, it inherits their lag — it is a follower, not a forecaster.

One quirk worth knowing: MACD is plotted in the price units of the stock, so a ₹4,000 stock will show much bigger MACD values than a ₹200 stock. That means MACD levels are not comparable across stocks the way RSI's 0–100 scale is — only the shape (crossings, the histogram's rise and fall, divergence) carries meaning, not the absolute number.

Key idea

MACD is just the distance between two moving averages, smoothed and plotted. Everything it shows — crossover, histogram, zero line — is a restatement of "how far has the fast average pulled away from the slow one, and is that gap growing or shrinking?"

The signal-line crossover

The headline signal: when the MACD line crosses above the signal line, momentum is turning up (bullish cross); when it crosses below, momentum is turning down (bearish cross). It is the MACD equivalent of a moving-average crossover, but faster, because it operates on the difference of averages rather than the averages themselves.

Here is one that worked beautifully. SBIN's MACD line crossed above its signal line on 11 September 2025 at a price of 823 — and crucially, the cross happened with MACD already above the zero line, in established bullish territory. Over the next 30 days the stock gained +12%.

One clean win. Now the universe. We measured 7,640 bullish MACD crossovers across the Nifty 50 over 16 years. The results:

  • 58% were positive after 30 days — barely better than a coin flip, and only a little above the +1.48%/20-day baseline drift the market gives you for free.
  • Only 37% beat +5% over those 30 days. Nearly two-thirds of bullish crosses failed to deliver even a modest gain.
Did you know

Across 7,640 bullish MACD crossovers on the Nifty 50, only 37% went on to gain more than +5% within 30 days, and just 58% were positive at all. The crossover by itself is close to a coin flip — it is a starting point for analysis, not a signal to act on alone.

The crossover is so common because the lines cross constantly — including dozens of times during any sideways grind, each one a potential whipsaw. That frequency is precisely why the standalone hit rate is so ordinary.

The zero line is the context

Here is the filter most beginners skip. The MACD line crossing zero is more meaningful than it crossing the signal line, because zero is where the 12-EMA and 26-EMA themselves cross — a genuine change in the medium-term trend.

  • MACD above zero = the fast average is above the slow one = the underlying trend leans up. A bullish signal-line cross here (as in the SBIN example) is with the trend.
  • MACD below zero = the trend leans down. A bullish cross down here is fighting the prevailing direction — a counter-trend bet.

You might expect "only take bullish crosses while MACD is above zero" to sharply improve results. It is a sensible instinct — and on this data it did not help. A MACD-above-zero filter on the bullish crosses did not meaningfully lift the win rate. The lesson is not that context is useless; it is that this one mechanical filter, alone, does not rescue a near-coin-flip signal. Real edge comes from combining the cross with structure — a level, a trend, volume — not from stacking another version of the same momentum reading.

Heads up

Do not assume a "smart" filter fixes a weak signal just because it sounds rigorous. Requiring MACD above zero before a bullish cross feels safer, but on the Nifty 50 it did not improve the outcome. Test the filter before you trust it; an untested rule is just a story.

The histogram: momentum's early tell

The histogram — MACD minus signal — is often the most useful part, because it changes before the lines actually cross. When bars are tall and growing, the two lines are diverging and momentum is accelerating. When the bars start shrinking while still positive, the lines are converging — momentum is fading even though no cross has happened yet. A shrinking histogram is an early warning that the current leg is losing steam, giving you a heads-up well ahead of the lagging crossover.

Tip

Watch the histogram for the change, not the absolute height. Bars that stop growing and begin to contract tell you momentum is decelerating — often the first crack in a move, visible before price rolls over and long before the signal-line cross confirms it.

MACD divergence

Like RSI, MACD can diverge from price, and this is arguably its most respected use. A bearish divergence: price makes a higher high while the MACD makes a lower high — the new peak was reached with less underlying momentum, hinting the trend is tiring. A bullish divergence: price makes a lower low while MACD makes a higher low — selling pressure is weakening beneath a still-falling price. Because MACD measures the acceleration of a trend, divergence on it is a clean visual of a move quietly running out of fuel. As always, divergence is a warning, not a dated trigger — it tells you to tighten risk and wait for price to confirm, not to reverse on the spot.

Strengths and blind spots

MACD's strength is genuine: in a trending market it keeps you reading momentum and trend at once, the histogram flags fatigue early, and divergence catches exhaustion. Its blind spot is equally real: in a sideways, choppy market the lines cross back and forth endlessly, each cross a small loss, and the indicator becomes a whipsaw machine. Built from lagging EMAs, it is always a step behind price — by design.

This is the recurring theme of every indicator in this module. RSI, MACD and the moving averages are all derived from the same price you can already see; they repackage it usefully, but none of them know the future, and each has a regime where it shines and a regime where it misleads. The 37% figure is not a knock on MACD specifically — it is a reminder that no single indicator, used mechanically, carries an edge on its own. The edge lives in combining a momentum read like MACD with trend, structure and disciplined risk control.

Real example

A grounded way to use MACD: let price and a moving average decide the trend first. In a confirmed uptrend, treat a bullish MACD cross as timing for a continuation entry near support — not as a reason to buy in isolation. Use the histogram to gauge whether momentum is still building, and divergence to warn you when to step aside. The cross is one input among several, never the whole decision — remember, alone it is barely better than a coin flip.

Quick recap

  • MACD is two EMAs distilled: MACD line (12-EMA − 26-EMA), signal line (9-EMA of MACD), and the histogram (the gap between them).
  • The signal-line crossover is the headline trigger but, across 7,640 Nifty 50 events, only 37% beat +5% in 30 days and 58% were positive — close to a coin flip alone.
  • The zero line gives trend context (above = uptrend, below = downtrend), but a mechanical MACD>0 filter did not improve the cross on this data.
  • The histogram changes before the cross — a shrinking histogram is an early warning that momentum is fading.
  • Divergence between price and MACD flags a trend losing fuel — a warning to manage risk, not a dated signal.
  • MACD shines in trends and whipsaws in ranges; it always lags, because it is built from lagging averages.

Moving averages, RSI and MACD all describe direction and momentum — but none of them measure volatility, the width of price's daily swings. Next, Bollinger Bands wrap price in a volatility envelope, and we will see why "touching the band" is one of the most misread signals in technical analysis.

toc.text: The zero line is the context

19.html:
Most indicators try to tell you which way price is going. Bollinger Bands do something humbler and more honest: they tell you how much price is moving. They wrap a moving average in two elastic lines that breathe in and out with volatility — squeezing tight when a stock goes quiet, flaring wide when it gets loud. That single idea, drawn well, quietly solves a problem most beginners never name: knowing when the market is coiled and when it is already running.

The trap is that the bands look like they give buy and sell signals. The upper line seems to scream "too high, sell"; the lower line seems to whisper "too low, buy". Sixteen years of Nifty 50 data says that reading is wrong often enough to bankrupt you. A close above the upper band on our universe still returned +0.58% over the next 10 days — positive, not negative. The bands measure volatility. They do not measure direction. Confuse the two and you will sell every winner the moment it gets strong.

What the bands actually are

Bollinger Bands, created by John Bollinger in the 1980s, are three lines:

  • A middle band — usually a 20-day simple moving average, the same smoothed trend line you met in the moving-averages chapter.
  • An upper band — the middle band plus two standard deviations of price.
  • A lower band — the middle band minus two standard deviations of price.

Standard deviation is just a measure of how spread out recent prices are. When a stock has been swinging hard, standard deviation is large, so the bands sit far apart. When a stock drifts sideways in a tight range, standard deviation shrinks and the bands close in. The width of the channel is the volatility, drawn to scale.

That ±2 standard deviation default is not magic. Statistically, if price moves were perfectly random and bell-curved, roughly 95% of closes would fall inside two standard deviations. Markets are not perfectly random, but the rule of thumb survives: most days, price lives inside the bands, and the band edges are where price is statistically stretched — not where it is finished.

from openalgo import ta

# upper, middle, lower bands: 20-period SMA, 2 standard deviations
df["bb_upper"], df["bb_middle"], df["bb_lower"] = ta.bbands(df["close"], 20, 2)
Key idea

The middle band is the trend; the gap between the outer bands is the volatility. Bollinger Bands are two indicators in one — a moving average you already understand, plus a live volatility gauge wrapped around it. Read them as a condition, never as a command.

The squeeze: when quiet precedes loud

The most useful thing the bands tell you is when they get narrow. A squeeze is a period where volatility has collapsed — the bands pinch together because recent closes have barely moved. Quiet markets do not stay quiet forever. Pressure builds, and when it releases, the move that follows is often sharp. The squeeze does not tell you which direction the release will go — only that the calm is unusual and a bigger move is statistically overdue.

You can read the squeeze straight off the chart by eye, but the precise way to measure it is bandwidth:

bandwidth = (upper band − lower band) ÷ middle band

A small bandwidth number means a tight squeeze. On the chart below, BEL's bandwidth compressed to roughly 4.7% on 4 September 2024 — an unusually tight coil after a long quiet stretch — before the bands expanded again as the next move developed.

Notice what the squeeze did not do: it did not point up or down. It said "this stock is unusually quiet, and quiet does not last." That is genuinely useful — it tells you when to pay attention — but it is only half a trade. You still need price itself, or another tool, to tell you which way to lean.

Did you know

A squeeze is a statement about volatility, not direction. The bands can pinch tight and then break down just as easily as up. Traders who assume a squeeze always resolves upward are reading a direction that simply is not there.

Walking the band

Here is where most beginners go wrong. In a strong trend, price does not politely return to the middle after touching an outer band. It clings to the band and rides it — a behaviour traders call walking the band. In a powerful uptrend, a stock can close near or above the upper band day after day, because the trend is so strong that "two standard deviations above average" keeps getting redefined upward.

If your rule is "sell when price touches the upper band," walking the band is the rule that destroys you. You would have sold the strongest part of the strongest moves, again and again, while the stock kept climbing. The band touch was not an exit signal. It was the signature of strength.

Heads up

"Price touched the upper band" is not a sell signal, and "price touched the lower band" is not a buy signal. In a trend, repeated touches of one band confirm the trend's power — the opposite of a reversal. Treating a band touch as a fade is one of the most expensive beginner mistakes in technical analysis.

Why a band touch is not a signal

We can put a number on this. Across the Nifty 50 universe over 16 years, days where price closed above the upper band went on to return +0.58% over the next 10 days — positive, and almost exactly in line with the average return of an ordinary day inside an existing uptrend. In other words, a close outside the upper band gave you no edge in either direction. It certainly was not the reliable top that the textbook picture implies.

Compare that to the course baseline: any random Nifty 50 day has averaged about +1.48% over the next 20 days. A band touch did not beat that baseline and did not invert it. It is a measure of where price is relative to its recent range — nothing more. If the bands gave free sell signals, the forward return after an upper-band close would be sharply negative. It is not.

Real example

On BEL's chart, watch what happens after the September 2024 squeeze resolves: as the move gets going, price repeatedly pushes the upper band rather than collapsing off it. Anyone who shorted the first upper-band tag — because the textbook said "overbought" — was fighting the very strength the band was advertising. The band edge marked conviction, not exhaustion.

Bandwidth as a volatility cycle

Step back and the bands reveal a rhythm: volatility cycles between quiet and loud, then back to quiet. Bandwidth makes that cycle visible as a single line you can watch:

  • Low and falling bandwidth — a squeeze; the stock is coiling; expect the calm to break eventually.
  • Rising, wide bandwidth — volatility has expanded; a move is underway; the easy, explosive part may be maturing.
  • Very wide, then contracting — the move is cooling; the stock is returning toward balance.

This is why Bollinger Bands pair so naturally with the volatility chapter idea of ATR (average true range) — both answer "how much is this thing moving right now?" rather than "which way?". Knowing the volatility regime helps you size positions and set stops sensibly: a tight-squeeze stock needs a different stop than one whose bands are blown wide open.

Pairing bands with other tools

Because the bands are silent on direction, they only become a decision tool when you combine them with something that does speak to direction. Sensible pairings:

  • Bands + trend. Use the slope of the middle band (the 20-day average) to know which way the wind blows, then use band touches as evidence the trend is strong, not as fades.
  • Bands + a level. A squeeze that resolves through a known support or resistance zone is far more meaningful than one that resolves into open space. The level tells you direction; the band told you the move was coming.
  • Bands + volume. An expansion out of a squeeze backed by a genuine surge in volume is more convincing than a quiet drift outside the band — exactly the conviction question the next chapter tackles.

What the bands should never be paired with is another volatility indicator doing the same job. Stacking Bollinger Bands, Keltner Channels and an ATR panel together is not three confirmations — it is the same idea, drawn three times, fooling you into false confidence. We will name that trap directly in the combining-indicators chapter.

Tip

A clean way to use the bands: let the middle band's slope tell you the trend, let a squeeze tell you a move is brewing, and let price breaking a real level tell you the direction. The bands set the stage; price and structure pick the play.

Reading the bands honestly

Put together, here is the honest job description for Bollinger Bands. They are a volatility instrument wearing a trend line down the middle. They excel at one thing — showing you when a stock is unusually quiet (squeeze) or unusually stretched (band touch) relative to its own recent behaviour. They are useless, even dangerous, as standalone buy and sell triggers, because price walks the band in exactly the trends you most want to hold, and a band touch carried no negative edge in the data at all.

Use them to answer "is this market coiled or running, calm or stretched?" — and then let price, levels and volume answer "which way, and how convincingly?" That division of labour is the whole skill.

Quick recap

  • Bollinger Bands are a 20-day moving average plus and minus two standard deviations — the middle line is trend, the gap between the outer lines is volatility.
  • A squeeze (narrow bands / low bandwidth) flags an unusually quiet market and a likely bigger move — but says nothing about direction. BEL squeezed to ~4.7% bandwidth on 4 September 2024 before expanding.
  • In a trend, price walks the band: repeated touches confirm strength, they do not signal a reversal.
  • A band touch is not a signal. A close above the upper band still returned +0.58% over 10 days in our data — positive, not a sell.
  • Bandwidth turns volatility into a watchable cycle: quiet → loud → quiet.
  • Pair the bands with a direction tool (trend, a level, volume) — never with another volatility indicator that says the same thing.

Bands tell you how much price is moving. The next chapter adds the other half of conviction — how many people are behind the move. We turn to volume, the market's fuel gauge.

toc.text: The squeeze: when quiet precedes loud
Why a band touch is not a signal

20.html:
Price tells you what happened. Volume tells you how many people meant it. A stock can rise on a thin, sleepy session where almost nobody traded, or rise on a session where the whole market piled in — same green candle, completely different conviction behind it. Volume is the one input on the chart that is not derived from price at all. It is the raw count of shares that changed hands, and it is the closest thing a chart gives you to a crowd's body language.

That makes volume powerful and also easy to over-read. A big volume bar feels important, so beginners treat every spike as a signal. The data is more careful. Volume confirms — it raises or lowers your confidence in a move price is already making. On a real test, breakouts backed by heavy volume failed less often than quiet ones, but they still failed plenty. And the biggest volume day of all can mean the opposite of a clean signal: a flood of trading with almost no price progress is two crowds fighting to a draw, not a green light.

Volume as conviction

Think of every candle as a vote and volume as the turnout. A 2% up-day on triple the usual volume means a large, motivated crowd of buyers overwhelmed sellers — that is conviction. The same 2% up-day on half the usual volume means price drifted up because nobody was around to sell, not because demand was fierce. Identical candles, opposite stories, and only the volume bar tells them apart.

This is why volume is read in relative terms, almost never in absolute terms. "Five million shares" means nothing on its own — it could be a quiet day for one stock and a record for another. What matters is volume compared to the stock's own recent average. A common, honest way to do that is to compare today against the 20-day average volume.

from openalgo import ta

df["vol_avg20"] = ta.sma(df["volume"], 20)
df["vol_ratio"] = df["volume"] / df["vol_avg20"]   # today vs its own 20-day norm

A ratio near 1.0 is an ordinary day. A ratio of 2.0 means twice the usual participation. A ratio above 5 or 6 is a genuine event — the kind of day where something happened.

Key idea

Volume is always read relative to a stock's own recent average, never as a raw number. The question is never "is this a lot of shares?" — it is "is this a lot for this stock, right now?"

Confirming breakouts and trends

Volume earns its keep at the moment of a breakout. When price pushes through a level the whole market is watching, the question is always: is this real, or a trap? You met the brutal base rate in the channels chapter — across the Nifty 50 over 16 years, 56% of 20-day breakouts faked out, falling back below the broken level within five days. More than half were traps.

Volume tightens those odds. Filter the same breakouts for volume greater than 1.5× the recent average, and the fakeout rate dropped from 56% to 47%, with better forward returns on the ones that held. A breakout on heavy volume says a real crowd is behind the push; a breakout on thin volume says price wandered through the level with nobody watching, and is far more likely to be reclaimed.

Did you know

Heavy volume on a breakout cut the failure rate from 56% to 47% in our 16-year Nifty 50 test — a real, useful improvement. But notice: nearly half of even the high-volume breakouts still failed. Volume tilts the odds in your favour. It does not guarantee the move.

In an established trend, the same logic runs quietly in the background. A healthy uptrend tends to see volume swell on the up-days and fade on the pullbacks — the crowd shows up to buy and loses interest to sell. When that flips — when rallies come on shrinking volume and selloffs come on rising volume — the trend is running out of fuel even if price has not yet broken. That is the early warning a price-only chart cannot give you.

Spikes, climaxes and churn

A volume spike is a single bar towering over its neighbours. It marks a moment when an unusual number of people had to act — earnings, news, a block deal, a stop-loss cascade. Spikes are where information enters the chart. But a spike is a question, not an answer, and the answer depends entirely on what price did on that same bar.

On the chart below, BEL traded about 6.9× its 20-day average volume on 27 September 2024 — an enormous spike, the sort of bar that stops you scrolling. The lesson is what you do next: you read the price action on and after that bar, because the same volume can mean very different things.

  • Spike with a strong directional close — a large crowd moved price decisively. That is conviction, in the direction of the close.
  • Spike near the end of a long trend (a climax) — when a stock that has run for months puts in its heaviest volume bar at a new extreme, it can mark exhaustion: the last buyers piling in at the top, or the last sellers capitulating at the bottom. The crowd's biggest day is sometimes its final one.
  • Spike with a tiny price move — churn. This is the one beginners misread. A huge-volume bar that closes almost exactly where it opened means buyers and sellers traded furiously and neither won. That is not a signal to act on; it is two crowds in a standoff. Enormous volume with no price progress is indecision at high intensity, not a launchpad.
Heads up

A record-volume day with almost no price movement is churn, not a breakout. Massive turnout that produces a flat candle means the market could not agree — a balanced fight, not a winner. Do not read conviction into a volume bar until you have checked what price did alongside it.

Volume divergence

Just as price and momentum can disagree, so can price and volume — and the disagreement is informative. Volume divergence is when price makes a new high (or low) but volume does not confirm it.

  • Price grinds to a fresh high while volume on each new push gets thinner: the rally is being carried by fewer and fewer participants. The trend is narrowing — a quiet caution flag.
  • Price slides to a new low but volume dries up rather than expanding: the selling is exhausting itself; the sellers who wanted out are mostly out.

Divergence is a warning, not a trigger. Like everything in this course, it shifts probabilities — it does not flip a switch. A trend on fading volume can still run for weeks before it turns. You use divergence to lower your conviction and tighten your risk, not to call the exact top.

OBV in plain words

On-Balance Volume (OBV) turns the "volume confirms direction" idea into a single running line. The recipe is simple: on every up-day, add that day's volume to a running total; on every down-day, subtract it. The result is a cumulative tally of whether volume has been flowing into the stock on up-days or out of it on down-days.

from openalgo import ta

df["obv"] = ta.obv(df["close"], df["volume"])

You do not read OBV's value — the number itself is meaningless. You read its slope and its agreement with price. When price makes a new high and OBV makes a new high too, buying volume is confirming the move. When price makes a new high but OBV refuses to follow, the up-days are coming on lighter volume than the down-days — the same divergence warning, packaged as one clean line instead of a row of bars.

Tip

Read OBV as a confirmation line, not a number. The only questions worth asking are: is OBV trending the same way as price (confirmation), or are they pulling apart (divergence)? Its absolute level tells you nothing.

Delivery vs traded volume in India

One detail matters specifically for Indian stocks. The volume bar on a chart is total traded volume — every share that changed hands, including pure intraday churn where a trader buys and sells the same shares within the session. A large part of a day's volume can be speculative round-trips that net to nothing by the close.

NSE also reports delivery volume — the portion of shares that were actually delivered into demat accounts, meaning the buyer kept them overnight rather than flipping them intraday. A high delivery percentage on a big-volume day suggests investors, not just intraday traders, were behind the move — generally a sign of stronger conviction. A heavy-volume day that is almost all intraday churn, by contrast, can evaporate the next morning. When you have it, delivery data is a useful second layer on top of the raw volume bar.

Real example

Two stocks both close up 3% on a 4× volume spike. One shows 70% delivery — investors accumulating and holding. The other shows 15% delivery — intraday traders flipping the same shares all day. The price candles look identical; the delivery split tells you which crowd was really there, and which move is more likely to stick.

Quick recap

  • Volume is the one chart input not derived from price — it counts how many people backed a move. Read it relative to the stock's own recent average, never as a raw number.
  • Volume confirms; it doesn't trigger. Breakouts on >1.5× average volume faked out 47% of the time versus 56% for all breakouts — a real edge, not a guarantee.
  • A spike marks forced action, but its meaning depends on price: a decisive close is conviction; a flat close on record volume is churn, not a signal.
  • BEL traded ~6.9× its 20-day average volume on 27 September 2024 — read the price action on the bar before assuming what a spike means.
  • Divergence (price up, volume fading) is a caution flag, not a trigger. OBV packages the same confirm-or-diverge question into one running line — read its direction, not its value.
  • In India, separate delivery volume from total traded volume: high delivery means real holders; heavy intraday churn can vanish overnight.

You now have four indicator families on the table — trend, momentum, volatility and volume. The temptation is to bolt all of them onto one chart and feel five times as confident. The next chapter explains why that feeling is a lie, and how to combine indicators without fooling yourself.

toc.text: Delivery vs traded volume in India

21.html:
Open any beginner's chart and you will find the same scene: a moving average, plus an RSI panel, plus a MACD panel, plus a stochastic, plus a second moving average, plus a momentum oscillator. Six indicators, all lit up green, and the trader feels six times as sure. It is one of the most comforting illusions in trading — and one of the most expensive.

Here is the problem. RSI, MACD, the stochastic and a rate-of-change oscillator are all built from the same raw material: recent price momentum. When they all flash "buy," they are not six independent witnesses agreeing. They are one witness, saying the same sentence in six accents. Stacking them does not multiply your confidence — it multiplies your blind spots, because every one of those tools is deaf to the same things: volume, volatility and the actual structure of price. Real confirmation comes from tools that look at different things. That is the entire point of this chapter.

The four families

Almost every indicator ever invented belongs to one of four families, defined by what question it answers:

Family The question it answers Example tools
Trend Which way is price generally heading? Moving averages (SMA, EMA), Supertrend, ADX
Momentum How strong and fast is the current move? RSI, MACD, stochastic
Volatility How much is price moving right now? Bollinger Bands, ATR, bandwidth
Volume How many participants are behind the move? Volume, OBV, volume average

The insight that changes how you build a chart is this: tools inside the same family are largely redundant; tools across different families are genuinely complementary. RSI and the stochastic are both momentum — pick one, not both. But a moving average (trend), RSI (momentum), Bollinger Bands (volatility) and OBV (volume) each look at a different dimension of the same chart. When those four agree, you have something close to real corroboration.

Key idea

The four families are trend, momentum, volatility and volume. Combine indicators across families, not within one. Five momentum oscillators all saying "buy" is one opinion repeated five times — not five confirmations.

Redundancy vs real confirmation

It is worth being blunt about why same-family stacking is actively harmful, not just useless. Three risks:

  1. False confidence. Five agreeing momentum tools feel like overwhelming evidence, so you size up and ignore the stop. But they were always going to agree — they share an input. You have manufactured conviction out of nothing and bet more on it.
  2. Shared blindness. Because momentum tools ignore volume and volatility, a wall of agreeing oscillators tells you nothing about whether the move has real participation behind it or whether the stock is so quiet that the signal is noise. The thing most likely to sink the trade is the thing none of your tools can see.
  3. Analysis paralysis. More panels means more chances for something to disagree, somewhere, on some setting. Traders with twelve indicators rarely take trades — there is always one red light — and when they do, they cannot tell you which signal they actually acted on.
Heads up

If two indicators almost always light up at the same time, they belong to the same family and one of them is clutter. Adding a second tool that says the same thing as the first does not strengthen the signal — it just hides how much you are really relying on one idea.

Trend + momentum + volume: a worked combination

A clean, classic combination uses one tool from three different families and asks them three different questions in sequence:

  • Trend (the filter): Is price above a rising long moving average? This decides whether you should be looking for longs at all. Trade with the trend, not against it.
  • Momentum (the timing): Within that uptrend, is momentum turning back up after a dip — say, RSI lifting off a low, or MACD curling up? This times the entry inside the trend you already qualified.
  • Volume (the conviction): Is the move you are entering backed by above-average volume? This tells you whether a real crowd agrees, or whether price is drifting on thin air.

Each tool covers a blind spot of the others. The trend filter stops momentum from buying counter-trend bounces that fail. Momentum stops you from buying a trend that has already run too far without a pause. Volume stops you from trusting a move nobody showed up for. That is corroboration — three different lenses, one picture.

Add a volatility read on top — Bollinger bandwidth or ATR — and you have all four families covered, which is also what sets your stop distance and position size sensibly.

from openalgo import ta

# one tool from each family, on the same dataframe
df["ema50"] = ta.ema(df["close"], 50)                 # trend: which way?
df["rsi"]   = ta.rsi(df["close"], 14)                 # momentum: how strong?
df["atr"]   = ta.atr(df["high"], df["low"], df["close"], 14)  # volatility: how much movement?
df["obv"]   = ta.obv(df["close"], df["volume"])       # volume: who's behind it?

Four lines, four families, zero redundancy. That is a complete read of a chart in four numbers — and it fits on one screen without clutter.

Tip

Before adding any indicator, ask: which family is it, and do I already own that family? If you already have a momentum tool and you are about to add another, stop. Spend the slot on a family you are missing — usually volume or volatility, the two beginners neglect most.

Indicators confirm; price leads

Now the most important sentence in the chapter, and the one that quietly undoes most indicator obsession: every indicator is calculated from price, so price always knows first. A moving average is an average of past prices. RSI is a ratio of past price changes. MACD is the difference of two price averages. None of them contain information that is not already in the price and volume bars — they are just re-presentations of that data, smoothed and scaled to be easier to read.

That has a hard consequence. An indicator can never lead price; it can only lag it, because it is built from price that has already happened. When a beginner waits for "MACD to confirm" before acting, they are waiting for a delayed echo of a move the candles already showed. Indicators are not a crystal ball bolted on top of the chart. They are a cleaner way of reading the chart you already have.

So the hierarchy is fixed: price and structure lead; indicators confirm. You read the trend, the levels and the candles first — that is the signal. Then you let one tool per family raise or lower your confidence in what price is telling you. An indicator should never override what price is plainly doing. If price has broken a major level on heavy volume and your oscillator says "overbought," price wins — you met that exact lesson in the RSI and Bollinger chapters, where "overbought" stayed overbought and band touches kept paying.

Did you know

Every indicator in this course is computed from price and volume — they add no new data, only a clearer view of existing data. That is why price leads and indicators confirm, never the other way round. A signal that exists only in an indicator and nowhere in the price is usually noise.

Avoiding indicator overload

The cure for overload is subtraction, not addition. A practical discipline:

  • One tool per family, maximum four. If you cannot justify an indicator by naming its family and the question it answers, it comes off the chart.
  • Default to fewer. Most professional charts carry one or two indicators, not eight. Trend plus volume covers an enormous amount of ground on its own.
  • Know what each one is blind to. A trend tool is blind to exhaustion; a momentum tool is blind to participation; a volatility tool is blind to direction; a volume tool is blind to where price is in its range. Your combination should patch the holes, not pile up in one corner.
  • Let disagreement mean something. When your trend tool and your momentum tool point opposite ways, that conflict is information — usually "the trend is intact but stretched, wait for the pullback" — not a reason to add a tie-breaker indicator.

A clean chart template

Put it together and a professional-feeling, honest chart needs surprisingly little:

  1. Price as candlesticks — the primary signal, always.
  2. Key levels — the support and resistance zones and trendlines you drew by hand.
  3. One trend tool — for example a 50-day moving average, to keep you on the right side of the market.
  4. One volume read — the volume bars with their 20-day average, or OBV, to gauge conviction.
  5. Optionally one momentum or one volatility tool — RSI or Bollinger Bands, for the dimension you care about most. Not both of everything.

That is it. Price and levels do the heavy lifting; three or four indicators, one per family, confirm. Anyone whose chart is busier than this should be able to name the family and job of every line on it — and most cannot, which is exactly why their charts are busy.

Real example

A trader watches BEL break above resistance. Price leads: the breakout is on the chart in plain candles. Trend confirms: price is above a rising 50-day average. Volume confirms: the breakout bar trades well above its 20-day average. Volatility frames it: the bands had squeezed beforehand, so an expansion was due. Four families, one decision, one clean chart — and not a single redundant oscillator in sight.

Quick recap

  • Indicators sort into four families — trend, momentum, volatility, volume — defined by the question each answers.
  • Tools within a family largely repeat each other; combine across families for genuine confirmation. Five momentum tools agreeing is one opinion, not five.
  • Same-family stacking breeds false confidence, shared blind spots and paralysis. The cure for overload is subtraction.
  • A clean combination uses one tool per family: trend filters, momentum times, volume confirms conviction, volatility sizes the risk.
  • Price leads; indicators confirm. Every indicator is computed from price, so it can only lag — it is a cleaner view of the chart, never a crystal ball on top of it.
  • A professional chart is mostly price and levels, plus three or four non-redundant indicators — and you should be able to name the job of every line.

You can now read a single chart from four angles at once. But one chart is one timeframe — and a stock can look bullish on the weekly and bearish on the daily at the same moment. Next, we go top-down: using multiple timeframes so the bigger picture and the entry agree before you ever act.

toc.text: Trend + momentum + volume: a worked combination

22.html:
The same stock can look like a textbook buy and a textbook sell at the very same minute. Open BEL on the weekly chart and you see a powerful, multi-year climb; drop to the 15-minute chart and you might catch it bleeding red, three down-candles in a row, RSI sinking. Both pictures are true. They just answer different questions.

That contradiction is not a flaw in the charts — it is the whole point of looking at more than one. The professional's habit is to never trade off a single timeframe. They zoom out to decide which way they want to lean, then zoom in to decide when to act. This chapter teaches that top-down routine: the higher timeframe for direction, the lower one for timing, and how to refuse the trades where the two disagree.

Why one chart is never enough

A timeframe is just the amount of price packed into each candle. On a daily chart, one candle is one trading day. On a weekly chart, one candle is a whole week — roughly five daily candles squeezed into one shape. On a monthly chart, one candle swallows about four weekly candles.

Here is the consequence that trips up beginners: a move that looks enormous on a small timeframe can be a single, meaningless wick on a large one. A 4% two-day drop feels like a disaster when you are staring at the hourly chart. Zoom out to the weekly and that same drop is one small lower shadow on a candle still making higher highs. Same price, same week, opposite emotion.

If you only ever look at one timeframe, you are blind to the context that decides whether your signal matters. The day-trader who shorts every red hourly candle inside a roaring weekly uptrend is fighting the tide. The investor who buys a "cheap dip" without noticing the weekly chart broke down months ago is catching a falling knife. Neither has done anything wrong on their own chart — they have just looked at only one.

Key idea

The higher timeframe is the ocean; the lower timeframe is the wave. You can surf the wave, but if you ignore which way the tide is running you will get dragged out. Direction comes from the big picture; timing comes from the small one.

The higher timeframe sets the direction

Start at the top. For a swing trader working off daily charts, the "higher timeframe" is usually the weekly. Your one and only job here is to answer a simple question: is this market in an uptrend, a downtrend, or going sideways? You are not looking for an entry. You are deciding which side of the trade you are even allowed to take.

You already have every tool you need from earlier chapters. On the weekly chart, ask:

  • Is price making higher highs and higher lows (up), lower highs and lower lows (down), or neither (range)?
  • Is it above or below a long moving average, and is that average sloping up or down?
  • Where are the major weekly support and resistance levels — the big walls price has to deal with?

Look at BEL on the weekly view and the answer is not subtle. From a low of 174.7 on 12 February 2024, the stock climbed to 468.4 by 6 March 2026 — a gain of 168.2%, built from a clean series of higher highs and higher lows. On the weekly timeframe, this is an uptrend, full stop. The top-down rule that follows is blunt: in a market that looks like this, you hunt for buys, not shorts. The tide is going up.

The lower timeframe times the entry

Once the weekly chart has told you the direction, drop to the daily to find the moment. The higher timeframe says "lean long"; the lower timeframe answers "but not at any price — wait for a pullback, a level, a trigger."

This matters because even the strongest uptrends do not go straight up. Inside BEL's 168% weekly advance there were dozens of daily pullbacks — stretches where the stock fell for days and every short-term indicator screamed weakness. Those daily dips are not the trend ending. In a higher-timeframe uptrend, they are the discount window: the place where you get to join the move at a better price than chasing a fresh high.

The data gives this a measured, honest edge. Across the Nifty 50, when a stock pulled back to a rising 50-day average, it returned +0.67% over the next 10 days, versus +0.58% for any day already in an uptrend. That is a real edge — but a thin one. A dip to a rising average is a slightly-better-than-average place to buy, not a magic floor that guarantees a bounce. The higher timeframe earns you the right to buy the dip; it does not promise the dip will hold.

Did you know

On the daily chart, BEL's 50-day average crossed above its 200-day on 12 May 2025 at 322.8, and the stock then gained 19.8% over the next 60 sessions. That entry worked partly because it agreed with the weekly trend - a long signal pointing the same way the bigger tide was already flowing.

This is also why you should resist trading the very lowest timeframes for direction. On the daily, BEL's Supertrend flipped 25 times in two years — twenty-five "change your mind" signals inside one clean weekly uptrend. The smaller the timeframe, the more noise you get and the more often you are whipsawed. Use the small chart to time, never to decide direction.

When the timeframes disagree

The most useful thing multiple-timeframe analysis does is tell you when to stand aside. The cleanest setups happen when the timeframes agree: weekly up and the daily giving you a pullback-and-turn into the trend. The most dangerous trades happen when they fight.

There are three states to recognise:

  • Aligned (act). Weekly uptrend, daily pulling back to support or a rising average and starting to turn up. This is your A-grade long. Both tides push the same way.
  • Conflicted (wait). Weekly up but daily breaking down hard, or weekly down but daily ripping up. One of them is lying to you, or a turn is genuinely underway and unconfirmed. The honest move is usually no trade — you have no edge when your own charts argue.
  • Aligned down (avoid or short). Weekly downtrend and daily weak. If you are long-only, this is simply "do not buy here," no matter how oversold and tempting the daily looks.

The classic beginner mistake is letting the exciting small timeframe override the boring big one. A thrilling daily breakout inside a weekly downtrend is exactly the kind of move that fails — you are buying a bounce in a market that wants to go lower. When in doubt, the larger timeframe wins, because it represents more money, more participants and more conviction.

Heads up

A signal on a lower timeframe that contradicts the higher timeframe is not a brave contrarian trade - it is usually just early and wrong. If the weekly says down and your 15-minute chart says buy, you are not smarter than the market; you are looking at noise. Demote the small chart to timing only.

Aligning weekly and daily in practice

You do not need three monitors and ten timeframes. For most swing traders, two are enough — one to set direction, one finer to time. A common, sensible pairing is weekly for the map, daily for the move. A pure intraday trader might shift the whole stack down a notch (daily for direction, 15-minute for timing), but the logic is identical: pick two timeframes about four-to-five times apart, and give each one job.

Computing this is straightforward. You can resample your daily data up to weekly and read the trend from there, then time the entry on the daily series:

from openalgo import ta

# Daily data in df, indexed by date
weekly_close = df["close"].resample("W").last()
weekly_trend = ta.ema(weekly_close, 20)      # weekly direction filter

df["ema20"]  = ta.ema(df["close"], 20)       # daily timing
df["sma50"]  = ta.sma(df["close"], 50)       # daily pullback reference

Read the weekly EMA's slope for direction; use the daily averages to find a pullback and a turn. The tools are the same ones you learned for a single chart — you are just applying them at two zoom levels and only acting when both agree.

A simple top-down checklist

Run this in order, top to bottom, before every trade:

  1. Weekly — direction. Up, down or sideways? Higher highs/lows? Above or below the long average, and which way is it sloping? This decides whether you may go long, short or neither.
  2. Weekly — the walls. Mark the major weekly support and resistance. Is price arriving at a wall (be cautious) or breaking free of one (be interested)?
  3. Daily — location. Is price at a sensible entry spot — a pullback to support or a rising average — or extended far from it? Never buy into thin air just because the weekly is up.
  4. Daily — trigger. Is there an actual signal to act on (a turn off the level, a confirming candle, a momentum cross) in the direction the weekly allows?
  5. Conflict check. Do the two timeframes agree? If they fight, the trade is "no." Stand aside.

Notice what this checklist quietly does: it stops you taking most trades. That is a feature. By the time a setup survives all five questions, you are entering with the bigger tide behind you and a defined spot to act — not chasing a single exciting candle on a single chart.

Quick recap

  • A timeframe is just price-per-candle; the same move can look huge on a small chart and trivial on a big one. Never decide off one chart alone.
  • Higher timeframe = direction. On the weekly, BEL ran +168.2% in a clean higher-high/higher-low uptrend — that decides which side you may trade.
  • Lower timeframe = timing. Use the daily to buy pullbacks into the trend; a dip to a rising 50-day average gave a thin +0.67%/10d edge, not a guaranteed bounce.
  • Smaller timeframes are noisier — BEL's daily Supertrend flipped 25 times in two years inside one weekly uptrend. Use small charts to time, never to set direction.
  • When timeframes disagree, the larger one wins and the honest move is usually no trade.
  • Run the five-step top-down checklist; its job is to reject most setups and only pass aligned ones.

You now know how to read direction and time an entry. Next, we turn that judgement into something you can test: a rule-based setup with an entry, an exit and a stop — and an honest backtest that tells you whether the edge is real.

toc.text: Why one chart is never enough
The higher timeframe sets the direction
The lower timeframe times the entry
Aligning weekly and daily in practice

23.html:
A setup is a sentence you could hand to a stranger and they would take the exact same trades you would. "Buy BEL when it looks strong" is not a setup — it is a feeling. "Go long when the close is above the 200-day average and the 50-day is above the 200-day; exit when that stops being true" is a setup, because there is nothing left to argue about. Every term is measurable, every decision is made in advance.

Turning a chart read into rules like that is what separates a hobby from a process. But writing the rules is the easy half. The hard, humbling half is testing them honestly against real history — and being willing to hear that your clever idea barely beats doing nothing. This chapter builds a simple rule-based setup, runs it across 48 real stocks, and reads the uncomfortable truth in the results.

From a hunch to a rule

A complete trading setup answers four questions, no exceptions:

  • Context — when am I allowed to even consider this trade? (the trend, the timeframe, the market regime)
  • Trigger — what specific event puts me in? (a cross, a breakout, a turn off a level)
  • Exit — what takes me out when I am right, and when I am wrong? (a target, a trailing rule, a stop)
  • Size — how much do I commit? (next chapter's job)

If any one of these is vague, you do not have a system — you have a story you will rewrite mid-trade to feel better. The discipline is to nail all four down before a rupee is at risk, so that in the heat of a live trade there is nothing to decide, only a rule to follow.

Our example setup is deliberately plain — a classic long-only trend filter:

from openalgo import ta

df["sma50"]  = ta.sma(df["close"], 50)
df["sma200"] = ta.sma(df["close"], 200)

# In the market only when both conditions hold; otherwise sit in cash.
in_trend = (df["close"] > df["sma200"]) & (df["sma50"] > df["sma200"])

The rule: hold the stock whenever the close is above its 200-day average and the 50-day is above the 200-day; otherwise, be flat in cash. It is about as simple as a system gets — one trend filter, no targets, no cleverness. That simplicity is the point, because a simple rule is something we can actually trust the backtest on.

Key idea

A real setup is a rule a stranger could execute identically. If your "system" needs you there to interpret it, it is not testable, not repeatable, and not a system - it is discretion wearing a costume.

Decide entry, exit and stop before you click

Notice that our rule defines the exit in the same breath as the entry. That is non-negotiable. The single most common way traders turn a winning idea into a losing account is by entering on a rule and exiting on an emotion — taking profits early out of fear, holding losers forever out of hope.

Three things must be fixed in advance:

  • Entry — the exact condition that puts you in. ("Both averages line up and price closes above the 200-day.")
  • Exit-when-right — what banks the win. A target, a trailing stop, or simply "stay until the trend rule breaks."
  • Exit-when-wrong — the stop. The price or condition that admits the trade has failed and gets you out small. (We size and place this properly in the next chapter.)

Our trend filter folds the stop into the rule: the day the conditions stop being true, you are out. Crude, but defined. The virtue is that you never have to decide during the trade — the decision was made when you were calm.

What a backtest actually shows

A backtest replays your rule over historical data and reports what it would have done. It is the closest thing technical analysis has to a laboratory — and it is where most beautiful ideas go to die. We ran our trend filter across 48 Nifty stocks over 16 years and compared it to the simplest possible alternative: buy the stock and just hold it.

The result is a masterclass in honesty.

Read those numbers slowly:

  • The trend filter beat buy-and-hold in only 4 of 48 stocks — about 8% of the time.
  • Median total return was +107% for the filter versus +744% for just holding. Holding won, and not by a little.
  • But the filter's median maximum drawdown was −47% versus −56% for holding — a shallower worst-case fall.
  • And it was invested only about 55% of the time, sitting safely in cash for the other 45%.

So the honest verdict on our clean, sensible, widely-taught trend filter is: it mostly underperformed simply holding the stock, while delivering a somewhat smoother ride and far less time exposed to the market. It is not a money machine. It traded a large slice of return for a smaller drawdown and more peace of mind. Whether that is a good deal depends entirely on what you value — but anyone who sold you this rule as a path to riches was not looking at the data.

Did you know

This is the recurring lesson of the whole course, now measured end-to-end: a golden cross was positive only 59% of the time at 60 days; MACD crosses beat +5% only 37% of the time; and now a full trend-filter backtest underperforms holding 92% of the time. The edge in trading was never in the signal. It is in risk control and discipline.

Reading the equity curve

The single most useful output of a backtest is the equity curve — the running value of the account over time. It shows not just how much you made but how you made it: the smooth stretches, the violent drawdowns, the long flat periods of doing nothing.

The RELIANCE curve tells the story behind the averages. The trend filter dutifully stepped aside during big declines — good — but because the rule needs the trend to re-establish before re-entering, it was sitting in cash through the early, explosive part of every recovery. Markets often make their largest single-day and single-week gains right off the lows, precisely when a lagging trend filter is still flat. Miss those, and you lag for years.

This is the trade-off no indicator can escape: a filter that protects you from the worst declines also locks you out of the best recoveries. Lag cuts both ways.

Win rate is not edge

Beginners obsess over win rate — the percentage of trades that profit. It is the most misleading number in trading. You can win 90% of the time and still go broke if the 10% of losses are huge, and you can lose 60% of the time and grow rich if the 40% of wins are large enough.

What actually matters is expectancy — the average rupee outcome per trade:

expectancy = (win% × average win) − (loss% × average loss)

A system that wins just 40% of the time but makes 2 rupees on winners for every 1 it loses on losers has a positive expectancy: (0.40 × 2) − (0.60 × 1) = +0.20 per rupee risked. It loses more often than it wins and still grinds upward. This is why the reward-to-risk ratio is the lever that pays. A trader who religiously cuts losers small and lets winners run can be wrong most of the time and still win overall.

Tip

Stop asking "how often does it win?" and start asking "what does it make per trade after costs?" A high win rate with tiny wins and rare giant losses is a slow bankruptcy. A modest win rate with disciplined reward-to-risk is a business.

The trap that ruins every backtest: curve-fitting

Here is the seductive danger. Our trend filter underperformed — so the temptation is to tune it. Try 47/210 instead of 50/200. Add an RSI condition. Skip trades in March. Keep twiddling and eventually you will find a combination that, on this exact history, beat buy-and-hold spectacularly.

You will also have built something worthless. That is curve-fitting (or overfitting): bending the rules until they perfectly explain the past, which guarantees nothing about the future. With enough parameters you can fit any historical wiggle — and a model tuned to old noise will be helpless against new noise. The market does not repeat; it rhymes, and a curve-fit system is memorising the verse instead of learning the rhyme.

The defences are simple and unforgiving:

  • Prefer fewer rules and round numbers. A 50/200 filter that you did not optimise is more trustworthy than a 47/213 filter that you did.
  • Test out-of-sample. Build the idea on one stretch of history, then check it on a stretch it never saw. A real edge survives the move; a curve-fit one collapses.
  • Test it across many instruments, as we did with 48 stocks — not on the one chart where it happened to look brilliant.
  • Be suspicious of perfection. A backtest with no losing years and a silky curve is almost always overfit. Real edges are lumpy and uncomfortable.
Heads up

If adding a rule makes the backtest much prettier, assume you are fitting noise until proven otherwise. The goal of a backtest is not to find the rule that fit the past best - it is to find a robust rule that will probably survive a future you cannot see.

From idea to a tested setup

Put the whole loop together:

  1. Write the rule as a sentence a stranger could execute — context, trigger, exit, all defined in advance.
  2. Backtest it honestly across many instruments and a long history, costs included.
  3. Read the equity curve and drawdown, not just the headline return — a smoother −47% beat a richer −56% for many traders' nerves.
  4. Resist tuning. If it only works after heavy optimisation, it probably does not work.
  5. Decide what you are buying — return, smoothness, time-out-of-market — and accept the trade-off with open eyes.

Quick recap

  • A setup is four answered questions: context, trigger, exit, size — all fixed before the trade.
  • Define entry, exit-when-right and exit-when-wrong in advance; the calm decision beats the heat-of-the-moment one.
  • Our simple 50/200 trend filter beat buy-and-hold in only 8% of 48 stocks; median +107% vs +744% — but with a shallower −47% drawdown and only ~55% time invested. A simple rule mostly underperforms holding while controlling the downside.
  • The equity curve reveals the trade-off: protection from the worst declines costs you the best recoveries. Lag cuts both ways.
  • Judge a system by expectancy and reward-to-risk, not win rate.
  • Curve-fitting makes the past look perfect and the future fail — prefer few rules, round numbers and out-of-sample tests.

A tested setup tells you what to do; it cannot tell you how much to risk on each trade — and that single decision matters more than the rule itself. Next, we put risk first: stops, reward-to-risk, the 1-2% rule, and the brutal math of drawdown.
