Decide entry, exit and stop before you click
The trap that ruins every backtest: curve-fitting
From idea to a tested setup

24.html:
Ask a new trader what they are working on and they will tell you about entries — the perfect pattern, the magic indicator, the signal that gets them in at the bottom. Ask a trader who has survived a decade and they will talk about something far less glamorous: how little they risk, where they get out, and how they make sure no single trade can ever take them out of the game.

That is the great inversion of this chapter. Profit is optional and unpredictable; survival is mandatory and entirely in your control. You cannot make the market go up, but you can decide exactly how much you lose when you are wrong — and that one decision, made before you enter, matters more than any signal in this course. We put risk first, because the trader who is still standing in five years is rarely the best analyst. They are the one who never let a bad trade end them.

Risk comes first

Most people build a trade in the wrong order. They find a setup, get excited, buy, and then — maybe — think about where to get out. By then they are emotionally committed and every stop feels like an insult to their brilliant idea.

Flip it. Decide the risk first, and let the risk decide everything else. The sequence that keeps traders alive is:

  1. Where will I admit I am wrong and get out? (the stop)
  2. How much money am I willing to lose if that happens? (the risk budget)
  3. Therefore, how many shares can I buy? (the position size)

Notice that position size is the last thing you calculate, not the first. You do not decide "I'll buy 500 shares" and then hope. You decide how much you are willing to lose, mark where the trade is wrong, and let the arithmetic tell you the size. Risk is the input; size is the output.

Key idea

You control three things in a trade and none of them is the outcome: where you get in, where you get out, and how much you bet. Master those and the wins take care of themselves. Obsess over the outcome and you will manage none of them.

The stop-loss: deciding where you are wrong

A stop-loss is a price at which you exit a losing trade, no questions asked. It is not an admission of failure — it is the cost of being in the game, like the small ante that lets you sit at the table. Every professional is wrong constantly; the difference is they are wrong small.

A stop should live where your reason for the trade is broken — not at some round number that feels comfortable. If you bought because price held a support level, the stop belongs just below that level: if support gives way, your thesis is dead and you want out. If you bought a pullback to a rising average, the stop sits below the swing low that the pullback should not breach. The market, not your wallet, decides where you are wrong.

Two cautions from earlier chapters apply directly. First, support and resistance are zones, not exact lines — place the stop a little beyond the level, not exactly on it, so normal noise does not knock you out of a trade that is still valid. Second, remember that oversold can get more oversold and overbought can stay overbought; a stop is what protects you when a level you trusted simply fails, as levels eventually do.

Heads up

A stop you move further away to avoid being hit is not a stop - it is a wish. The entire point is that you set it when you are calm and honour it when you are scared. Widening it mid-trade is how a small, planned loss becomes the giant, unplanned one that does real damage.

Reward-to-risk: only take trades that pay you to be right

Before you take a trade, measure two distances: from your entry to your stop (the risk, R), and from your entry to a realistic target (the reward). Divide them and you get the reward-to-risk ratio.

Say you buy at 322.8 with a stop at 300 — you are risking 22.8 per share. If a sensible target is 380, you stand to make 57.2. That is a reward-to-risk of about 2.5-to-1: you risk 1 to make 2.5. This single ratio is what makes win rate almost irrelevant. As the last chapter showed, a trader who wins only 40% of the time but keeps reward-to-risk above 2:1 still comes out ahead, because the wins are bigger than the losses. Cut losers at 1R and let winners run to 2R or 3R, and the math works for you even when you are wrong more often than right.

The practical rule: be picky. If a setup only offers a target a hair above its stop — a reward-to-risk below 1:1 — it is rarely worth taking, however pretty the chart. You would need to win the large majority of such trades just to break even, and few setups do.

The 1-2% rule

Here is the rule that quietly does most of the work of keeping accounts alive: never risk more than 1-2% of your trading capital on a single trade.

Risk, here, means the money you lose if your stop is hit — not the position's full value. On a 10,00,000 account, a 1% risk budget is 10,000 per trade. That is the most a single trade is allowed to cost you. With that cap in place, a string of losses is survivable and unremarkable: even ten consecutive losers, a genuinely terrible run, dents the account by roughly 10%, not 50%. You live to trade the eleventh.

Compare that to the trader risking 20% a trade. Three losses in a row — entirely normal, as our backtests showed signals fail more often than they succeed — and they are down nearly half their account, staring at the recovery math we are about to see. The 1-2% rule is not timidity. It is what makes a losing streak an annoyance instead of an ending.

Tip

The 1-2% is a budget, not a target. You are not trying to lose 1% - you are ensuring that if you do, it barely registers. Small, fixed risk is the single habit most strongly shared by traders who are still around years later.

Position sizing from the stop

Now the three pieces snap together into one formula. Once you know your risk budget and your stop distance, the number of shares is fixed — no guessing:

capital   = 1_000_000          # Rs 10,00,000
risk_pct  = 0.01               # risk 1% per trade
entry     = 322.8              # BEL entry
stop      = 300.0              # below the level you'd be wrong at

risk_amount   = capital * risk_pct          # Rs 10,000 you can lose
risk_per_share = entry - stop               # Rs 22.8 at risk per share
qty = int(risk_amount / risk_per_share)     # 438 shares

The arithmetic: 10,000 of allowed loss, divided by 22.8 of risk per share, is about 438 shares. That position is worth roughly 1,41,000 — about 14% of the account — yet if the stop is hit you lose only the planned 10,000, your 1%.

The beautiful part is what happens automatically. A tight stop lets you hold a bigger position; a wide stop forces a smaller one — and your money-at-risk stays constant either way. A volatile stock whose stop must sit far away gets sized down; a calm stock with a close stop gets sized up. You are no longer guessing how many shares "feels right." The stop and the 1% rule decide it for you, and the decision is the same whether you are calm or terrified.

The cruel math of drawdown

Why all this fuss over small losses? Because losses and the gains needed to recover them are not symmetric — and the gap gets vicious as losses grow. A loss of X% requires a larger gain to climb back, because you are now recovering from a smaller base.

Loss taken Gain needed just to break even
−10% +11%
−20% +25%
−33% +50%
−50% +100%
−75% +300%
−90% +900%

Read the bottom of that table and feel it. A 50% loss does not need a 50% gain to recover — it needs 100%, a full double, just to get back to where you started. Lose 75% and you must quadruple what is left. This is the mathematical reason deep drawdowns are so deadly: they do not just hurt, they steepen the climb out to something most traders never achieve, and they do it while your confidence is at its lowest.

This is also the real argument for risk management over chasing returns. Recall the last chapter's backtest: the trend filter's median worst drawdown was −47% against buy-and-hold's −56%. From −47% you need about +89% to recover; from −56% you need about +127%. Shaving the drawdown was worth real money on the way back up — which is exactly why a smoother, shallower equity curve can beat a higher-returning but deeper one for a trader who has to live through it.

Did you know

The math of recovery is why the 1-2% rule and stops are not optional extras. A trader who never lets a drawdown get deep never faces the brutal end of that table. The whole discipline of risk management is, at heart, a campaign to keep your losses in the top two rows.

Putting it together

A complete, risk-first trade plan, in order:

  1. Find the setup (context, trigger) — from everything in this course.
  2. Mark the stop where your thesis would be wrong — below the level, beyond the noise.
  3. Check reward-to-risk — is the target at least ~2x the stop distance? If not, pass.
  4. Set the risk budget — 1-2% of capital, in rupees.
  5. Size from the stop — risk budget ÷ (entry − stop) = shares. This is the only place your position size comes from.
  6. Execute and honour the stop — the decision was made when you were calm; follow it when you are not.

Every step except the first is about defence. That is not pessimism — it is the recognition that you cannot control whether a trade wins, only how much it can cost you when it loses.

Quick recap

  • Risk comes first: decide the stop and the loss you can accept before the trade, and let those decide the position size.
  • A stop-loss belongs where your reason for the trade is broken — a little beyond the level, never moved further away to dodge a hit.
  • Reward-to-risk above ~2:1 lets you profit even with a sub-50% win rate; skip trades that pay less than they risk.
  • The 1-2% rule caps each trade's loss so a losing streak is survivable, not fatal.
  • Size from the stop: risk budget ÷ (entry − stop) = shares — a tighter stop allows more shares, a wider one fewer, with rupees-at-risk held constant.
  • Drawdown math is merciless: −50% needs +100% to recover. Keeping losses shallow is the whole game.

You now have the analysis, the system and the risk math. One variable remains, and it is the one that breaks most traders: the person executing the plan. Next, the last edge — psychology, discipline, and where to go from here.

toc.text: The stop-loss: deciding where you are wrong
Reward-to-risk: only take trades that pay you to be right

25.html:
You can finish this entire course — read every candle, draw every level, memorise every base rate — and still lose money. Not because the analysis failed, but because in the live moment, with real rupees on the line, you did not do what you knew to do. You held the loser "just a little longer." You skipped the stop because the chart "felt" like it would bounce. You chased the breakout you swore you would wait for.

That gap, between knowing and doing, is the last and largest edge in trading. Every chart skill in this course is available to everyone with an internet connection; what is scarce is the discipline to apply them when it is uncomfortable. This closing chapter is about the one variable no indicator can compute — the person in the chair — and then it points you toward what comes next: turning a tested setup into a system that runs whether or not your emotions cooperate.

The three saboteurs: fear, greed and FOMO

Almost every self-inflicted trading wound traces back to three emotions, each one inverting a rule you already know.

  • Fear makes you cut winners early and freeze on losers. You sell a good trade the moment it shows profit, terrified of giving it back — then you sit paralysed as a loser drifts past your stop, because closing it makes the loss "real." Fear gets the reward-to-risk math exactly backwards: small wins, big losses.
  • Greed makes you oversize and overstay. After a couple of wins you risk 5% instead of 1% "because you are hot." You hold past your target hoping for more, and watch the gain evaporate. Greed is what turns the careful sizing of the last chapter into a single bet that can hurt you.
  • FOMO — the fear of missing out — makes you chase. A stock gaps up 6% on news and you buy at the high, abandoning your plan, because watching it run without you is unbearable. Recall the data: more than half of 20-day breakouts fail back within five days, and bigger gaps fill less but run harder — chasing extension is precisely the low-odds trade.

The cruel design is that all three feel like action, like you are doing something smart. In reality each one overrides a rule you set when you were calm. The market is an engine for converting your emotions into other people's profits.

Heads up

Your worst trades will feel the most justified at the time. Revenge-trading after a loss, doubling down on a "sure thing," chasing a runaway move - these never feel like mistakes in the moment. They feel like conviction. That is exactly why you need rules written down before the moment arrives.

Why most traders lose

It is no secret that the large majority of active retail traders lose money over time — study after study, in India and abroad, finds the same uncomfortable result. The interesting question is why, and this course has quietly answered it, chapter by chapter, with data.

The losses are not mainly because people pick bad indicators. They are because the edges are thin and the mistakes are expensive:

  • The signals barely beat doing nothing. The baseline drift of any Nifty 50 day was already +1.48% over 20 days. A golden cross was positive only 59% of the time at 60 days. A full 50/200 trend filter beat simple buy-and-hold in just 8% of 48 stocks. The honest edge in a single signal is small — and small edges are easily erased.
  • Costs and overtrading erase the thin edge. Brokerage, taxes and slippage are subtracted from every trade. Trade constantly, chasing every wiggle, and you pay those costs over and over until a slim edge goes negative.
  • Emotion inverts the math. Cutting winners and riding losers flips reward-to-risk against you; oversizing turns a normal losing streak into the deadly bottom rows of the drawdown table.
  • Curve-fitting and false confidence. Stacking five indicators that all say the same thing, or tuning a system until the past looks perfect, feels like rigour and delivers ruin.

Put together: a small real edge, minus costs, minus emotional errors, minus overtrading, lands most people below zero. The losers are not lacking charts. They are lacking the discipline and risk control that protect a thin edge long enough for it to pay.

Key idea

Most traders do not lose because the market is unbeatable. They lose because they take a small statistical edge and then give it all back through oversizing, overtrading, chasing, and refusing to take stops. The edge was never the problem. The behaviour was.

Discipline over prediction

Here is the reframe this whole course has been building toward. Beginners think trading is about prediction — being right about where price goes next. The data says prediction is a coin-flip-plus-a-little: a 59% signal, a 56% fakeout rate, a +2.2% best-bucket return. Nobody is reliably right.

Professionals do not try to be right. They try to be disciplined: to take many small, defined-risk bets where the odds are slightly in their favour, cut the losers without argument, let the winners run, and repeat the process thousands of times so the thin edge compounds. The edge is not in the next trade. It is in following the same sound process across hundreds of trades while everyone around you is improvising.

This is genuinely good news, because discipline is learnable in a way that prediction is not. You will never reliably forecast a stock. You can absolutely learn to honour a stop, size every trade the same way, and not chase. That is a skill, and it is the one that pays.

The written plan

Discipline does not survive contact with a live, moving market unless it is written down beforehand. A trading plan is your calm self leaving instructions for your panicked self. It does not need to be long — it needs to be specific enough that there is nothing to decide in the heat of the moment:

  • What I trade — which instruments, which timeframe, which setups (and nothing else).
  • My entry — the exact condition, top-down, that puts me in.
  • My stop and target — where I am wrong, and a realistic reward-to-risk I require before taking the trade.
  • My size — risk 1-2% of capital, sized from the stop, every time.
  • My rules of conduct — no trading after a certain number of losses in a day; no chasing gaps; no widening stops; no trade that breaks the plan.

The power of the plan is that it converts trading from a series of fresh, emotional decisions into the simple act of following or not following a rule. When a trade tempts you off-plan, you do not have to win an argument with yourself — you only have to notice it is not in the plan.

The trading journal

If the plan is the rulebook, the journal is the game tape. After each trade, record what the setup was, why you took it, where your stop and target sat, and — most importantly — whether you followed your plan, regardless of whether the trade won or lost. Note the emotion you felt.

This separation is the whole point. A good trade is one where you followed your rules; a bad trade is one where you broke them. The outcome is almost beside the point: a winning trade taken on a whim is a bad trade that happened to pay, and it will teach you a habit that eventually costs you dearly. Over a few dozen entries, the journal stops lying to you. It shows the patterns — the FOMO chases, the moved stops, the oversized "sure things" — that no amount of charting skill will fix until you can see them.

Tip

Grade yourself on process, not profit. Tally how many of your last twenty trades followed your written plan exactly. If that number is low, your problem is not your charts and no new indicator will fix it - your edge is leaking through your own hands.

From reading charts to running systems

There is a structural reason discipline is so hard for a human: you have to feel fear and greed in real time and override them, trade after trade, forever. There is a way to take much of that weight off your shoulders — and it is the natural next step from everything you have learned.

Once a setup is written as clear rules (Chapter 23) with risk defined (Chapter 24), it no longer needs you to pull the trigger. It can be backtested rigorously over years of history, then automated so the rules execute exactly as written — no chasing, no moved stops, no skipped entries. The machine does not feel FOMO.

This is exactly what OpenAlgo, the free and open-source platform behind this course, is built for. The same openalgo library you have seen computing indicators on a Rust core can be wired into a complete workflow: pull real NSE market data, compute your signals, test the setup against history, run it first in sandbox trading (analyzer mode) so you can validate behaviour with no money at risk, and then connect it to your own account for live execution — all with your rules in code instead of your emotions in charge. Automation does not invent an edge you do not have, but it does protect a real edge from the one variable most likely to destroy it: you.

Did you know

The honest promise is not that automation makes you money - the backtests in this course are proof that simple rules are no money machine. The promise is that automation makes you consistent: it executes a tested, risk-managed plan the same disciplined way on your worst day as your best. Consistency is what lets a thin edge survive long enough to matter.

Your path from here

You began this course unable to tell a candle from a bar. You can now read market structure, mark levels that matter, judge a pattern in context, use indicators without drowning in them, and — most importantly — you know the base rates: which classic "rules" the data supports and which it quietly debunks. That honest, evidence-first foundation is rarer than any pattern.

Where you go next depends on what pulled you in:

  • To turn these chart skills into running, automated strategies, continue with the other free courses at /learn — they cover Python for traders, algorithmic and quantitative trading, and building systems on OpenAlgo, picking up exactly where this course's bridge to code leaves off.
  • To deepen the analysis itself, return to any chapter and re-run its study on stocks you follow. Every chart in this course came from real NSE data you can pull yourself; test the claims again, because a fact you have verified with your own hands is one you will actually trust under pressure.

A final word. Technical analysis is not a crystal ball, and this course never pretended otherwise — that honesty was the point. What it gives you is a disciplined way to read probability and control risk: to take sensible bets, lose small when you are wrong, win bigger when you are right, and stay in the game long enough for a modest edge to compound. The charts are the easy part. The discipline to follow them is the craft of a lifetime — and you now have everything you need to begin it well.

Quick recap

  • Fear, greed and FOMO each invert a rule you already know — cutting winners, oversizing, chasing extension — and each one feels like smart action.
  • Most traders lose not from bad charts but from giving back a thin edge through costs, overtrading, and emotional errors; the edge was never the problem, the behaviour was.
  • Aim for discipline, not prediction — a learnable skill that compounds a small edge across many trades.
  • A written plan made when calm, plus a journal that grades you on process rather than profit, are the tools that build that discipline.
  • The next step is to backtest a tested, risk-managed setup and automate it with OpenAlgo — running it in sandbox trading (analyzer mode) first — so consistency replaces emotion.
  • Continue your journey with the free courses at /learn, and keep verifying every claim against real data with your own hands.

That is the course. You came to read charts; you leave knowing how to read odds, manage risk, and act with discipline — the difference between someone who watches the market and someone who can trade it. Go well.
